title: Gate on fast-tier p99 and standard-tier p95
preview: Summary: This PR adds tier-aware SLO gating to perf-canary for the Workload-aware Batching Defaults launch. We now fail the perf-canary run if: (1) FAST tier p99 latency regresses beyond threshold (tail-latency protection gate), or (2) STANDARD tier p95 latency regresses beyond threshold (steady-state gate). The intent is to stop merges that silently violate the published tier SLOs while still allowing legitimate improvements to land with a controlled baseline refresh process. Motivation: As we move batching config from ad-hoc per-route knobs to three customer-facing tiers (fast / standard / cost-efficient), we need CI guardrails that match what we’ll ship. Previously, canary gating was based on a single aggregate p95 and a single scenario per model. That missed fast-tier tail regressions (p99) and could overreact to noise in standard tier when concurrency ramps changed. What changed (high level): (1) Adds a tier_slo_gates.yaml config describing which percentile to gate on per tier, thresholds, and the scenario → tier mapping. (2) Extends the results parser to ingest tier labels from benchmark-lab output (scenario metadata), and compute per-tier regression deltas against the stored baseline. (3) Adds a baseline refresh workflow with explicit approvals, including a short “baseline justification” note that is stored next to the refreshed baseline file. (4) Adds a regression triage runbook tailored for tiered batching: how to distinguish scheduler regressions vs workload-mix drift, and what artifacts to attach to the Linear issue. Details: Gating rules: FAST tier: gate on p99 end-to-end latency, primary metric = request_latency_ms (includes queue + prefill + decode), scenario group = mixed_workload_streaming_shortprefill + mixed_workload_nonstreaming_shortprefill. Threshold default = +6% relative regression OR +25ms absolute (whichever triggers first) with a minimum sample size requirement. STANDARD tier: gate on p95 end-to-end latency, scenario group = mixed_workload_balanced + summarization_mediumprefill. Threshold default = +5% relative regression OR +35ms absolute. Note: COST-EFFICIENT tier is intentionally not a merge-blocking gate in this PR; we still compute and report it, but it only posts a warning annotation. This matches the staged rollout plan and avoids blocking on known variance for aggressive batching experiments. Noise controls: (a) sample-size gate: if we have < N requests for a tier/scenario, the gate is “insufficient data” and does not fail the run (but posts a warning). (b) warm-up exclusion: ignore first K requests in each scenario to reduce cold-start variance. (c) optional “max allowed stddev” heuristic (disabled by default) included behind a flag for future tuning. Baseline refresh process (new): We now store baselines under baselines/<model_family>/<hardware_class>/baseline.json and include a matching baselines/<...>/baseline.meta.txt with: timestamp, perf-canary run URL, benchmark-lab revision, and a one-paragraph human summary of why the refresh is safe (e.g., “intentional latency improvement due to scheduler tweak”). To refresh: run ./scripts/refresh_baseline.sh --model-family llama --hw h100 --scenario-set tiers --run-id <canary-run> then open a PR containing only baseline files + meta. That PR must have approvals from one Runtime reviewer and one SRE reviewer. Regression triage notes (new docs): Added docs/regression_triage_tiered_slo.md. Key guidance: verify the tier label in the artifact, check queue depth & batch-size distribution in the attached benchmark-lab plots, compare streaming vs non-streaming deltas (fast-tier p99 is usually driven by admission/wait budget), and tag the resulting Linear issue with runtime+slo-gate. Also includes “when to refresh baseline” vs “when to block merge and fix” decision table. Changed areas/files (high level): - config/tier_slo_gates.yaml: new canonical gating config - src/gating/tier_slo_gate.py: new tier-aware gate implementation - src/results/parser.py: scenario metadata → tier mapping + percentile extraction - src/cli.py: new flags for tier gating, warm-up exclusion - scripts/refresh_baseline.sh: baseline refresh helper + guardrails - docs/baseline_refresh.md: refresh procedure + required approvals - docs/regression_triage_tiered_slo.md: triage playbook - .github/workflows/perf-canary.yml: upload tier gate summary, annotate PRs Commit summary: 1) parser: ingest scenario metadata and emit tier breakdown 2) gating: implement fast p99 + standard p95 gates w/ thresholds 3) scripts/docs: add baseline refresh + triage docs 4) workflow: upload artifacts + PR annotations Validation: - Ran on 6 recent canary runs for top model families; no false failures after warm-up exclusion and sample-size checks - Verified that a known fast-tier p99 regression (synthetic +12% added wait budget) fails the gate - Verified that a known standard-tier improvement (scheduler prefill/decode split change) passes and baseline can be refreshed cleanly Follow-ups (not in this PR): - Add an optional gate for COST-EFFICIENT tier once variance is reduced (likely after we stabilize queue backpressure heuristics) - Integrate “reason codes” (profile selection telemetry) into the canary report when available from runtime 1.22 - Expand coverage to additional regions/hardware classes once Dedicated fleet baselines exist

title: add adaptive outlier suppression to canary evaluator
preview:
Motivation: Canary evaluators occasionally trigger rollbacks due to short-lived outliers (e.g., transient downstream hiccups or probe skew) that do not reflect sustained regressions. This PR introduces an adaptive outlier suppression layer that reduces sensitivity to isolated spikes while preserving detection of real regressions.

Summary of changes: Implemented a three-stage suppression strategy integrated into the existing canary scoring pipeline: 1) lightweight per-probe z-score normalization (robust to small sample sizes), 2) median-filtered spike-dampening that discounts single-sample deviations based on recent windowed variance, and 3) Bayesian weight recalibration that downweights probes with recurring discordant signals across slices. The suppression is parameterized and feature-flagged so operators can tune aggressiveness per-customer or per-route.

Why this is safe: The suppression is applied only at the evaluator decision layer (after metric aggregation) and does not alter raw metric ingestion or storage. The default configuration is conservative (minimal suppression) and must be enabled per-environment via rollout flags. We added comprehensive unit and integration tests and a canary job to validate behavior under synthetic noise.

Commits (high-level): - Initial implementation of suppression module and unit tests - Integrate suppression into canary evaluator pipeline and expose feature flag - Add integration test harness and synthetic noise scenarios - Dashboard panels and runbook update - Tuning iteration after reviewer feedback

Files changed (high-level): - pkg/evaluator/suppression.go: core suppression algorithms + docstrings and benchmarks - pkg/evaluator/pipeline.go: hook points to call suppression before scoring - pkg/evaluator/suppression_test.go: unit tests covering edge cases and small sample behavior - integrations/noise-harness/: synthetic traffic generator and canary job config - docs/runbooks/canary-outlier-suppression.md: operational notes and tuning guide - dashboards/canary/suppression-overview.json: Grafana panel definitions summary - go.mod / go.sum: updated deps for probabilistic utils - scripts/ci/integration_canary.sh: new CI job to run noise scenarios

Behavioral details: - Default window: 5m sliding window, min samples=30 for full Bayesian recalibration. - For windows with <30 samples the module uses robust z-score thresholds to avoid overfitting. - The median-filter dampening multiplies a per-probe anomaly weight by (1 - alpha) where alpha adapts to observed variance; alpha in [0.0,0.5] by default. - Weight recalibration applies a Beta-prior update to probe reliability scores; probes that consistently disagree with other probes have their weight reduced smoothly rather than clipped.

Compatibility and rollout: - Feature-flagged under env CANARY_SUPPRESSION_MODE with values {off, conservative, aggressive}. - Default for hosted perf-canary is conservative to minimize behavioral drift. - Observability added: per-canary suppression_ratio and per-probe adjusted_weight metrics exported with same cardinality as existing metrics to avoid downstream changes.

Testing and CI: - Unit tests cover small-sample and large-sample regimes, edge-cases where all probes disagree, and reliability decay scenarios. - Integration test runs the synthetic noise harness that simulates 10k requests with injected 2-10s latency spikes and verifies no increase in false-positive rollback rate compared to baseline. - CI: initial run failed due to flaky integration timing; added retries and deterministic seed in harness; subsequent CI run passed (see CI summary below).

Performance: Benchmark micro-optimizations added to suppression.go to keep evaluator p95 CPU overhead < 1% on our standard canary worker. Local benchmarks included in package bench tags; results attached to PR artifacts.

Runbook: docs/runbooks/canary-outlier-suppression.md includes instructions for toggling the flag, recommended tuning for bursty vs. steady traffic, and how to inspect adjusted_weight and suppression_ratio metrics when diagnosing missed regressions.

Review thread (selected): - Oliver Grant: \"Can we ensure this never masks a sustained 10% latency regression?\" Author: \"Yes — the Bayesian recalibration decays weights gradually; we also expose suppression_ratio across time. Conservative mode sets alpha max to 0.15 to keep sensitivity.\" - Priya Natarajan: \"How does this interact with multivariate canaries (latency + error rate)?\" Author: \"Suppression runs per metric slice; we keep cross-metric correlation signals intact and only adjust probe weights per metric. Added a test case where error-rate and latency diverge.\" - Ethan Liu: \"CI was flaky; add deterministic seed please.\" Author: \"Added deterministic seed for the harness and retries; re-ran CI and green.\" - QA comment: \"Add dashboard panels and add to SLO playbook.\" Author: \"Included dashboard JSON and updated runbook; requested product review for dashboards.\"

CI summary: - unit: pass - lint: pass - integration-noise-harness: failed (first run) -> fixed timing, deterministic seed -> passed on rerun - e2e (canary pipeline): pass with conservative mode enabled in test cluster

Merge outcome: Squash-merged into main. Merge commit: "suppress: adaptive outlier suppression for canary evaluator (squash)". Changelog: internal perf-canary: improved false-positive resilience under noisy traffic.

Follow-ups and TODOs: - Monitor suppression_ratio for first 2 weeks in canary clusters (ENG-4128). - Add telemetry-based autotuning prototype that will adjust alpha based on traffic entropy (separate PR). - Consider exposing per-tenant defaults in Dedicated offering after initial observational period.

Related work: contrasts with previous probabilistic smoothing PRs by focusing on probe-weight recalibration and per-window conservative defaults rather than global smoothing kernels.

If reviewers want a walk-through, I can demo the noise harness and dashboard panels in the next engineering sync.

title: add-rolling-entropy-degradation-fence
preview:
Summary: Introduces a Rolling Entropy Degradation Fence for canary rollouts. Motivation: percentile-based checks (p50/p95/p99) miss subtle distributional shifts where tails shift gradually or mass moves between latency modes. This PR implements an adaptive entropy-based detector that measures changes in the latency/throughput histogram distribution over a rolling window and fences rollouts when sustained distributional degradation is detected.

Design and behavior: The detector bins observed latency samples into an adaptive histogram (dynamic bucket sizing based on observed percentiles) and computes a rolling Shannon entropy plus a KL-divergence/JS-distance to a baseline histogram. We combine three signals: (1) instantaneous entropy change, (2) KL divergence to baseline, and (3) a sustained drift score computed with exponential decay. A canary fence is tripped only when the composite score exceeds the configured threshold for a configurable duration (default 5 minutes) and when traffic-weighted significance passes a minimal-sample check. The fence triggers: (a) pause rollout (canary traffic frozen), (b) escalate to oncall via alert channel, (c) automatically snapshot recent traces for post-mortem.

Implementation notes: - New module perf_canary/entropy_fence.py implements histogram maintenance, entropy/KL computation, smoothing and scoring. - A lightweight online estimator avoids storing raw samples; uses reservoir sampling for rare-event amplification in tail buckets. - Exposes config knobs via canon canary config: window_seconds, bucket_count, min_samples, entropy_threshold, kl_threshold, sustained_seconds. - Integration with existing rollout-control API: rollout_guard.plug('entropy_fence') returns decisions with reason codes and signal payload for observability. - Dashboard: dashboards/entropy_fence.json added with example panels (entropy timeseries, KL, composite score, sample counts). - Tests: unit + small integration tests under tests/test_entropy_fence.py and a synthetic load test script scripts/load/entropy_sims.py.

Compatibility & ops: Defaults are conservative (low false-positive rate). Gate-added to default canary guard chain but disabled for legacy customers via config flag. No schema changes to persisted canary objects. New IAM role not required.

Risks & mitigations: Entropy can be noisy on low-sample traffic; we added min_samples gating and sample-weighted smoothing. We also include a backoff and auto-unfreeze after manual-confirmation window if team marks event as false-positive.

Testing & validation: Local unit tests + simulated traffic scenarios included. We ran a two-week shadow run on internal staging (release/2026-02 staging) and verified zero false positives on normal traffic, detected 3 injected drift scenarios (gradual tail shift, bimodal shift, traffic-mode flip) and produced actionable signal.

Notes: This approach is complementary to existing percentile and trajectory detectors; recommended to use together for improved sensitivity to silent shifts.

title: implement signal smoothing and traffic-granularity guard for noisy tail regressions
preview:
Motivation: Recent canary runs showed recurrent false-positive tail-latency alerts during short traffic spikes, and we observed noisy variance for small-batch inference workloads. This PR introduces a two-part defense: 1) a lightweight temporal signal smoother that reduces high-frequency noise in per-route latency fingerprints while preserving changepoints, and 2) a traffic-granularity guard that slices canary evaluations by traffic orthogonals (route, payload-size bucket, and client tier) and gates rollouts when small, concentrated cohorts drive apparent regressions.

Goals: reduce alert churn, avoid unnecessary rollbacks for bursty micro-cohorts, and improve detection power for sustained regressions. This is intended as a conservative enhancement — default behavior is opt-in per-alert and the rollout guard has configurable thresholds and cooldown periods.

What changed (high-level): - Add exponential moving quantile smoother (EQS) and heteroskedasticity-aware window sizing to the canary metric stream processor. EQS adapts window length based on recent variance estimates so long-lived trends are preserved but transient spikes are suppressed. - Add traffic-granularity slicing to the canary evaluator. Evaluations now compute cohort-level fingerprints for route/payload_size/client_tier and will only escalate if either (a) a majority-weighted cohort shows sustained degradation, or (b) a global signal exceeds a tunable severity threshold. - New staged-fallback policy: on cohort-only degradation, the orchestrator applies a soft cork (reduce canary traffic by N%) and schedules a strengthened probe set; on global degradation the system triggers standard rollback. - Exposed config knobs in canary config and Redwood Console: smoothing_alpha, variance_thresholds, cohort_min_samples, staged_cork_percentage, staged_cork_cooldown. - Unit + perf tests and a small runbook for oncall to interpret cohort-level alerts.

Checklist:
- [x] Implementation of EQS and cohort slicing in canary processor
- [x] Unit tests for smoothing logic and cohort aggregation
- [x] Integration test with synthetic burst traffic profile
- [x] Console schema changes + docs entry
- [x] Runbook and alert text updates

Backward compatibility and safety: defaults preserve existing behavior when smoothing_alpha=0 and cohort_min_samples is large. The cohort guard is disabled by default for customers not enrolled in perf-canary v2.

Related: Linear ENG-1847 (reduce false positive canary alerts), ENG-1912 (cohort-aware canary rollouts), SUP-4123 (customer support ticket tying to bursty small-batch inference).

title: Add stratified noise calibrator and adaptive probe scheduler for low-signal canaries
preview: Context: Our canary detector has been producing noisy alerts for low-volume / heterogenous traffic slices (short queries, long queries, browser vs API) leading to unnecessary rollbacks. Motivation: reduce false positives and speed up meaningful detection in low-signal cohorts without increasing probe cost. This PR introduces two complementary components: (1) Stratified Noise Calibrator (SNC) — a per-stratum robust noise estimator that derives baseline variance and Minimal Detectable Effect (MDE) using median-absolute-deviation + IQR trimming and an empirical noise model; and (2) Adaptive Probe Scheduler (APS) — a probe allocation layer that dynamically assigns probe budget to strata based on expected information gain (posterior variance reduction) and the user's rollout priority hints. Approach: SNC computes noise baselines per stratum (user_cohort, model_variant, sequence_length_bucket) and emits metrics (perf_canary.noise_baseline, perf_canary.mde_estimate). APS queries SNC and current traffic rates, computes expected value-of-information, then increments probe rates for strata where MDE > configured detection target or where posterior uncertainty dominates. Both pieces are integrated into the rollout guard: when aggregate confidence is below threshold, APS will increase probes up to a capped budget and, if uncertainty persists, the guard will hold or throttle the canary. Statistical protection: SNC applies Benjamini-Hochberg FDR correction across strata to control multi-comparison false alarms and exposes a confidence score to the guard. Implementation notes: core logic implemented in Go in go/perf/canary; metrics and alerts via Prometheus rules; simulator and integration scenarios in py/tools/canary_simulator to reproduce 0.5%–5% regressions per cohort. Tests: unit tests for SNC math, APS allocation policy, and a small-scale end-to-end simulation that runs in CI (simulated traffic with deterministic RNG seeds). Backwards compatibility: default behavior is off; rollout policy must opt-in via canary.config.alpha.enable_stratified_calibrator = true. Checklist: [x] design doc linked (Linear ENG-5421) [x] unit tests [x] integration simulator scenarios [x] Prometheus recording rules and alert templates [x] docs and runbook [x] migration notes for existing canary dashboards. Risks & mitigations: metric cardinality — strata keys are canonicalized and capped to configured cardinality; noisy short-lived cohorts — APS applies minimum probe windows to avoid churn. Open questions: tune default MDE multiplier for PLG customers (left conservative).

title: Add latency-cliff detector and adaptive-concurrency rollout guard
preview: Motivation: We've observed hard 'latency cliff' failure modes where p95/p99 jump sharply within a short window even though rolling averages look stable; these events bypass percentile-based canaries and lead to partial outages. This PR implements a latency-cliff detector that detects rapid percentile deltas across short windows and wires an adaptive-concurrency rollout guard that temporarily reduces per-replica concurrency and pauses rollouts when a cliff is detected. Changes include detector algorithm, integration with the canary evaluation pipeline, a lightweight local load harness for reproducible cliffs, dashboards, and unit/integration tests. Checklist: [x] detector algorithm and unit tests, [x] canary integration and gating, [x] adaptive-concurrency policy and actuator, [x] dashboards and runbook, [x] CI flake fixes. Rollout plan: rollout as a guarded feature flag for 2 weeks, enable in perf-canary staging, monitor metrics and KB/alerts, then enable for canary->prod rollouts. Backwards compatibility: no public API changes; the guard is opt-in via canary config; defaults are off. Performance cost: detector is O(1) per incoming metric update and uses bounded memory. Related: ENG-1428, ENG-1534. Test plan: unit tests cover algorithm edge cases; integration test injects synthetic cliff using examples/load-profiles/stepped-cliff.yaml; manual verification with dashboard in charts/perf-canary-dashboard.json.

title: Add stochastic concurrency fence and staggered probe scheduler to reduce probe-induced noise
preview:
Context and motivation: We observed a recurring class of false-positive canary regressions when probe traffic arrives in short bursts or when client-side concurrency spikes align with canary probe windows. These cases amplify tail latency and create high variance in the canary signal, causing unnecessary rollbacks and developer churn. This PR implements two complementary mitigations: a stochastic concurrency fence (probabilistic admission control) to smooth bursty concurrent requests and a staggered probe scheduler to desynchronize probe timing across hosts.

What this changes (high level):
- Add perf-canary detector that computes `concurrency_entropy` per host and enables a probabilistic cap when entropy exceeds threshold.
- Replace existing probe dispatcher with a staggered scheduler that shards probe time windows to hosts and backfills missed probes without amplifying traffic.
- Export new prometheus metrics and tracing spans for debugging (see docs/designs/stochastic_fence.md).

Checklist: [x] design doc (perf-canary/docs/designs/stochastic_fence.md) [x] unit tests for fence and scheduler [x] integration stress test (tests/integ/canary_stress.sh) [x] backward-compatible config defaults [x] feature-flagged rollout in console

Implementation notes: The stochastic fence uses a lightweight token counter per runtime worker plus a small RNG-based drop decision to probabilistically reject new speculative requests once the measured concurrency distribution entropy rises above a configured baseline. The fence is parameterized by window_ms, entropy_threshold, and drop_p_max; drop probability grows with measured excess entropy to avoid hard cuts. The staggered probe scheduler assigns a deterministic offset per host based on host-id hashing and a jitter seed; it cooperates with the fence so probes don't get entirely rejected.

Safety and rollout: The feature is behind `perf.canary.stochastic_fence` flag and defaults to disabled in release builds. The canary pipeline now evaluates `perf_canary.concurrency_entropy` during pre-rollout shoulders and will auto-abort rollouts if entropy increases > 2x baseline and tail-p95 latency deviates > 20ms. We added a soft-fallback that disables the fence automatically if overall throughput drops > 5% relative to baseline.

Testing and benchmarks: Microbenchmarks show a 40% drop in variance for p99 under synthetic burst traffic and no measurable throughput regression (<1% difference) with default params. Integration stress runs ran for 2 hours across multiple hosts; one flaky e2e test failed initially due to an off-by-one in the offset calculation (fixed in commit 3) and CI was rerun.

Related: ENG-8421 (noise-reduction effort), ENG-8502 (canary signal stability). Design doc: perf-canary/docs/designs/stochastic_fence.md.

Please focus reviews on: correctness of entropy calc, safety of the fallback heuristics, and Prometheus naming/labels.

title: Add billing meter regression scenarios for streaming, retries, and fallback routing
preview:
Motivation: We’ve had multiple meter-related regressions where request-level behaviors (stream disconnects, server-side retry/fallback, and client retry with idempotency) caused double-counting or inconsistent attribution between (a) usage events and (b) the billing ledger. Unit/integration tests in the monorepo cover code paths, but we were missing an end-to-end canary that validates metering invariants against a live stack (gateway -> inference -> usage event emitter -> aggregation/export) under realistic failure modes.

This PR adds new perf-canary scenarios that intentionally trigger streaming + retry + fallback behaviors and then validate the expected metering invariants by querying the usage API / internal usage-events view within a bounded time window.

What’s included (new scenarios): 1) streaming_disconnect_no_double_count: Start a streaming chat completion, consume first N chunks, then terminate the client connection. Expectation: billed_tokens matches tokens actually generated before disconnect; no second usage event is emitted during cleanup; request_count increments exactly once; retry_count is 0. 2) server_retry_fallback_single_bill: Force a server-side retry/fallback by routing the request through a fault-injection profile (first attempt returns 5xx from upstream; second attempt succeeds). Expectation: the request is billed once; usage_event_id is stable across attempts (or deduped via attempt_group_id); raw_tokens may reflect both attempts in internal debug fields but billed_tokens reflects only the successful attempt; retry_count == 1. 3) client_retry_idempotent_key_dedup: Send a request with an Idempotency-Key, intentionally time out client-side before completion, retry with same key. Expectation: either (a) second call returns cached result and no additional billing occurs or (b) second call resumes and still produces a single billable event; duplicate_event_rate stays 0 for this request_id. 4) fallback_routing_region_attribution: Trigger a policy-based fallback from primary region to secondary region (capacity constraint simulation). Expectation: billing uses the final served region; region dimension is present and consistent across usage event + usage export; no “split” billing across regions for a single request.

Meter invariants validated: - Exactly-once billing per idempotency scope: sum(billed_tokens) and count(distinct billable_request_id) do not increase with retries/fallback beyond the expected single billable unit. - Streaming semantics: billed_tokens are derived from generated content that was successfully produced/committed, not from partial transport artifacts. - Attribution completeness: region, model, account_id, and endpoint are non-null; fallback routing uses the served_region dimension. - Dedupe signals: duplicate_event == false for all scenario request_ids; attempt_group_id groups retries.

Implementation notes: - Adds a small “meter_assert” helper that polls the usage API for the scenario request_ids with exponential backoff (default 8 minutes) to account for async event processing. - Uses deterministic scenario tags appended to metadata (x-redwood-canary-scenario + x-redwood-canary-run-id) so Finance/Eng can locate runs in Snowflake/Grafana during incident triage. - Adds fault-injection hooks via existing canary config (no new prod dependencies). - Adds a lightweight JSON snapshot written to canary artifacts (expected vs observed totals) for debugging and historical comparison.

Rollout/ops: - These scenarios are initially gated behind CANARY_ENABLE_METER_SCENARIOS=true in staging and canary-prod. - Alerting is set to “warn-only” for 1 week (no paging) until we establish baseline flakiness and tune the polling window.

Related: - redwood/pr-27463 (fix double-counting on stream retry) - redwood/pr-27518 (shadow billing compare mode) - redwood/pr-27485 (usage API: billed_tokens + cache_hit fields) - ENG-4430 (bug: prevent double-counting on retry path) - PM-1872 (metering change control / shadow period requirement)

Testing: - Local: make test (scenario unit tests) - Staging: ran canary suite x3 with fault injection enabled; no invariant violations; max usage-event lag observed 3m12s. - CI: lint + go test + docker build pass.

Notes for reviewers: Please focus on (1) invariant definitions (do we agree on billing semantics under streaming disconnect + retries), (2) polling/timeout defaults to avoid flake, and (3) scenario tagging fields to ensure they won’t leak to customer-visible metadata.

title: Introduce sequential probe compositor and adaptive cutover allocator
preview: Motivation: improve canary probe efficiency and reduce probe-induced noise during rollouts by composing sequential probes and allocating cutover budgets adaptively based on recent telemetry. This change introduces a Sequential Probe Compositor that stitches short-window synthetic probes into prioritized sequences to better surface phase-aligned regressions, and an Adaptive Cutover Allocator that computes per-rollout cutover budgets using traffic-conditioned stability scores and SLO-weighted credits. Key goals: lower false positives from probe collisions, preserve signal fidelity for tail latency, and enable safer gradual cutovers for large traffic shifts. Implementation notes: compositor merges probe windows across latency, throughput and error-rate signals; it maintains ordering and backpressure semantics so probes do not overload healthy replicas; allocator uses EWMA of percentile deltas and a stability-credit ledger to decide cutover percentages and cooldowns; both components expose Prometheus metrics and tracing hooks for downstream canary evaluators. Testing & rollout: added unit tests for sequence stitching and allocator math, integration harness that replays historical traffic traces to validate conservative/default budgets, and e2e traffic-driven scenarios validating fallback to existing sentinel policy. Checklist: [x] design doc referenced (ENG-2201); [x] unit + integration tests; [x] telemetry & dashboards; [x] docs/recipes update; [x] staged rollout to staging clusters. Related: ENG-2201, ENG-2283. This PR is a functional addition to perf-canary and is backwards-compatible with existing probe configs; default behavior remains unchanged until opt-in is enabled on rollout policies.

title: Add rolling resilience-curve canary with tiered fallback and rate fallthrough
preview: Motivation: We observed slow, multi-hour regressions that did not trigger existing spike-focused detectors; this PR adds a time-weighted 'resilience curve' canary that measures cumulative degradation slope across cohorts and triggers tiered safeguards. High level: 1) rolling resilience curve evaluator that computes a decay-weighted degradation area under curve (AUC) for latency and error rate across multiple cohort slices (region, client-class, route). 2) temporal quorum voting: requires consensus from multiple independent cohorts before a hard fallback. 3) tiered fallback policy: soft fallback (traffic shaping + alert only), model-variant fallback (route to a lower-cost stable variant), and rate fallthrough (graceful request shedding with progressive SLAs). 4) adaptive probe scheduling that increases probe frequency for cohorts showing rising AUC and backs off when stabilized. Checklist: - [x] design doc linked (Linear ENG-18721) - [x] unit + integration tests - [x] e2e smoke in perf lab - [x] docs added under docs/perf - [x] fallthrough behavior validated with canary simulator Implementation notes: The resilience curve is implemented in canary/engine/resilience_curve.py with an efficient incremental aggregator and configurable half-life. Voting is implemented as a quorum module that accepts weighted cohort votes. Fallbacks are implemented in alerts/rollout_guard.go and routing/soft_fallback.rs. This PR also adds a canary simulator harness and two smoke tests. Related: builds on prior probe-scheduling work (ENG-17234). Risk/rollback: feature gated behind canary.v2 flag; rollout guard defaults to soft mode until explicitly enabled in the console. Testing: see test/integration/resilience_smoke_test.py and perf-canary lab run id perf-lab-20260212-07. Merge notes: squash-merged to main after addressing review comments and CI fixes.

title: Add TTFT/TPOT threshold profiles for FP8/INT8 kernel canaries (per-SKU baselines + noise suppression)
preview: Context: As part of the FP8/INT8 kernel benchmarking sprint, we’re enabling quantized kernel modes behind flags and rolling out via perf canary. The existing canary gating uses a single threshold policy that was tuned on baseline (FP16/BF16) runs and has been noisy for quantized modes (especially when sample sizes are small or when the scheduler mix shifts TTFT/TPOT differently). This PR introduces explicit threshold profiles for quantized kernels and per-SKU baselines so we can gate safely and reduce false positives while still catching real regressions. Summary of changes: (1) Add threshold profiles keyed by kernel_mode (baseline|fp8|int8) and sku (h100|a100|l4) with separate TTFT/TPOT constraints and minimum sample requirements. (2) Add noise suppression: a small-sample guardrail, robust aggregation using trimmed mean for p95 deltas, and an EWMA-based stability check that prevents flip-flopping when the signal is borderline. (3) Add a per-SKU baseline registry: baseline references are pinned by (model, sku, region, traffic_profile) so the canary compares fp8/int8 vs the correct baseline cohort rather than a global default. (4) Add explicit “capacity/variance” classification: if GPU clocks are not locked or if variance exceeds the configured bound, canary emits a WARN and does not auto-rollback (still pages on-call if error rate spikes). Default policies included (initial cut): H100 fp8 is allowed tighter TPOT deltas but slightly looser TTFT due to known launch/scheduler interactions in mixed workloads; A100 int8 is looser on TPOT until we finish shape guardrails coverage; L4 has stricter min-sample and longer evaluation window to smooth bursty workloads. Config format: new YAML profiles under config/threshold_profiles/ with schema validation in CI. Example snippet (illustrative only): profile: quantized_h100_fp8; ttft_p95_delta_pct_max: 6; tpot_p95_delta_pct_max: 3; min_requests: 2000; ewma_alpha: 0.2; trimmed_mean_pct: 10. Operational notes: This PR does NOT change any runtime flags. It only changes how perf-canary evaluates telemetry deltas when kernel_mode is fp8/int8. Rollback behavior: auto-rollback triggers remain enabled only when (a) min_requests met, (b) stability check passes (not high variance), and (c) delta breaches the configured bound for 2 consecutive windows. Otherwise we emit a blocking canary failure (no rollout progression) but do not auto-flip flags. Follow-ups: (a) once ENG-7428 matrix runs are complete we should tighten H100 fp8 TTFT; (b) add model-family overrides for known long-context TTFT sensitivity; (c) wire in the new metrics dimensions (model/gpu/kernel_mode) after runtime PR lands (tracked in ENG-7427/PR-19934 in monorepo).

Default policies included (initial cut): H100 fp8 is allowed tighter TPOT deltas but slightly looser TTFT due to known launch/scheduler interactions in mixed workloads; A100 int8 is looser on TPOT until we finish shape guardrails coverage; L4 has stricter min-sample and longer evaluation window to smooth bursty workloads. Config format: new YAML profiles under config/threshold_profiles/ with schema validation in CI. Example snippet (illustrative only): profile: quantized_h100_fp8; ttft_p95_delta_pct_max: 6; tpot_p95_delta_pct_max: 3; min_requests: 2000; ewma_alpha: 0.2; trimmed_mean_pct: 10. Operational notes: This PR does NOT change any runtime flags. It only changes how perf-canary evaluates telemetry deltas when kernel_mode is fp8/int8. Rollback behavior: auto-rollback triggers remain enabled only when (a) min_requests met, (b) stability check passes (not high variance), and (c) delta breaches the configured bound for 2 consecutive windows. Otherwise we emit a blocking canary failure (no rollout progression) but do not auto-flip flags. Follow-ups: (a) once ENG-7428 matrix runs are complete we should tighten H100 fp8 TTFT; (b) add model-family overrides for known long-context TTFT sensitivity; (c) wire in the new metrics dimensions (model/gpu/kernel_mode) after runtime PR lands (tracked in ENG-7427/PR-19934 in monorepo).

title: introduce adaptive sensitivity scheduler for canary detectors
preview:
Motivation: long-running canary evaluators are sensitive to traffic volatility and changing baseline variance; fixed detection thresholds either generate noisy false positives during churn or miss subtle regressions when traffic is stable. This PR implements an Adaptive Sensitivity Scheduler (ASS) that continuously tunes detector sensitivity based on short-term traffic variance, cohort churn, and historical false-positive rate.

What it does (high level): introduces a scheduler that adjusts detector thresholds and aggregation windows at runtime. The scheduler observes three signals: instantaneous request-rate volatility, percentile drift across 1/5/15-minute windows, and the detector's empirical false-positive count. Using a lightweight Bayesian update, it adapts the detection sensitivity multiplier and the sample window size with safety bounds to avoid oscillation. Integrations added: canary evaluator policy (gates sensitivity changes behind rollout guard), telemetry hooks for new metrics (sensitivity_multiplier, scheduled_window_sec, scheduler_confidence), and opt-in feature-flagging via canary config.

Key design points: (1) Conservative priors: initial sensitivity is a small multiple of baseline to prevent overreaction after deploy spikes. (2) Stability hysteresis: changes are rate-limited and smoothed with exponential decay to avoid flip-flopping when traffic oscillates. (3) Safety gates: sensitivity cannot exceed an upper bound derived from SLO burn-rate budgets; if upper bound is reached scheduler forces immediate pause and raises a canary-alert event. (4) Auditability: scheduler emits human-readable decisions and rationale to the canary run log for postmortems.

Files & code: new scheduler implementation lives in perf-canary/scheduler/adaptive_scheduler.py with a lightweight C++-backed hot path for real-time metrics aggregation (perf-canary/scheduler/fastagg.cc) and Python bindings. Canary policy integrations are in perf-canary/evaluator/policy.py with new hooks to consult scheduled sensitivity before scoring. Configuration schema is extended: perf-canary/config/schema.yaml and charts/canary-helm/values.yaml include feature flag and bounds. Unit and integration tests added under tests/test_adaptive_scheduler.py and tests/integration/canary_scheduler_flow.test.

Testing and benchmarks: ran synthetic workloads (steady, bursty, seasonal) across canary clusters. Observed 47% reduction in false-positive rate on bursty traffic, with less than 3% decrease in true-positive detection rate for sustained regressions. Latency overhead on critical path measured <0.3ms per evaluation due to the fastagg hot path. Added CI job that runs targeted scheduler unit tests and a small end-to-end canary simulation (simulated 10k requests).

Rollout plan: feature gated off by default. Begin canary rollout to internal perf-canary staging clusters (10% of internal traffic) for 48h, monitor sensitivity_multiplier, scheduled_window_sec and scheduler_confidence metrics. If metrics look stable (no increase in false-positive rate and no sustained sensitivity upper bound hits), enable for production read-only (telemetry only) then enable active mode after one week. Provided a rollback script and documented runbook in perf-canary/ops/runbooks/ASS-runbook.md.

Why unique: unlike prior work which focused on fixed heuristics or static traffic-shape profiles, this scheduler continuously adapts to observed variance and links sensitivity to SLO budget. It is tailored for real-world LLM inference traffic where short-lived bursts and long-tail shifts are common.

Notes: this PR intentionally keeps the scheduler implementation modular so it can be replaced with a learned policy later (see ENG-7402). Backwards compatibility: canary config defaults preserve existing behavior when the feature is disabled.

title: Add quantile-weighted, phase-aware throughput fence and severity scoring
preview:
Problem: Existing canary detectors surface aggregate latency shifts but often miss multi-phase regressions where different traffic slices degrade at different times. We need a detector that (1) understands traffic phase boundaries (time-of-day, model warmup phases, routing slices) and (2) produces a single severity score that reflects both quantile skew and the persistence of the shift so rollout guards can make informed decisions.

This PR introduces a quantile-weighted, phase-aware throughput fence and a severity scoring function that folds into the canary evaluator pipeline. The detector computes quantile deltas per phase window and applies an exponential decay weighting across recent windows to emphasize sustained regressions while suppressing short-lived bursts. The severity score is a monotonic mapping of weighted-quantile deltas to [0,100] and is used by policy engines to trigger soft-fallback, pause, or hard-rollback depending on thresholds.

Key design points:
- Phase detection: configurable sliding window with heuristic phase boundary markers (traffic-rate change, model variant switch, time-partitioning). Phase rules are pluggable and default to traffic-rate heuristic.
- Quantile weighting: compare target percentiles (p50/p90/p99) against baseline, compute signed deltas, and transform them with a per-quantile sensitivity factor; combine into a single delta vector.
- Temporal aggregation: recent phase deltas are aggregated with exponential decay alpha (configurable) to compute sustained score.
- Severity mapping: configurable breakpoints to map numeric score to severity levels (info/warning/critical).
- Integration: detector implements the Detector interface and plugs into the existing canary evaluator; no changes required to routing or rollout policies other than optional configuration knobs.

Why different: unlike percentile-fusion detectors that treat traffic as a single homogeneous stream, this approach explicitly segments by phase and weights quantile violations to produce a severity that more closely tracks user-visible impact and avoids noisy short-lived triggers.

Behavioral knobs added in values and config:
- phase_window_seconds (default 300)
- lookback_phase_count (default 8)
- decay_alpha (default 0.4)
- quantile_weights: map {50:0.2, 90:0.5, 99:0.3}
- severity_thresholds: {warning: 25, critical: 60}

Testing and verification:
- unit tests for quantile delta calc, decay aggregation, and severity mapping included.
- local canary lab harness run against synthetic traffic with phase shifts (see test fixtures) demonstrating >92% detection rate for sustained 30% p90 regressions and <6% false positive rate on bursty noise.
- performance: detector adds ~1.3% CPU overhead in our canary agent under baseline load and 4MB additional RSS per agent.

Migration: default configuration preserves current canary behavior; operators can opt-in by enabling the detector through perf-canary config. Documentation added (docs/design/quantile-weighted-detector.md) and chart values exposed for helm deployments.

Checklist: 
- [x] Implementation of detector and integration with evaluator pipeline
- [x] Unit tests and CI coverage
- [x] End-to-end canary lab runs with synthetic traffic profiles
- [x] Documentation and helm values
- [x] Telemetry: new metrics prefixed with perf_canary.qw_ for score, phase_count, and per-quantile-delta

Related: SUP-4012 incident follow-up that motivated phase awareness; ENG-2145 for long-running canary reliability improvements.

Notes: This change is additive and opt-in. I expect additional tuning of quantile_weights and decay_alpha after broader rollout.

title: Add Quality Gate enforcement check stage to perf-canary pipeline
preview:
Background: Optimize now requires quality gates before applying cost-saving config diffs (batching/cache/quantization). We’ve had a near-miss during beta where enforcement drifted between services (Optimize apply endpoint blocked correctly, rollout promotion didn’t). This PR adds a dedicated perf-canary stage that continuously verifies gate enforcement semantics end-to-end against a controlled staging route, and alerts on regressions.

What this adds:
- New canary stage: `quality_gate_enforcement`
  - Executes an end-to-end scenario against staging Optimize + eval-harness + rollout-controller API surface.
  - Validates enforcement at the apply boundary (blocked when gate != PASSED) and at promotion boundary (canary promotion blocked on failed gate).
- A small, deterministic test fixture:
  - Uses a dedicated canary account + route (non-production) and a synthetic config diff that is expected to FAIL the gate (known-bad quant profile on long-context prompts).
  - Uses a separate config diff that is expected to PASS smoke gates (benign batching window tweak) to ensure the stage itself isn’t only testing the fail path.
- Result reporting:
  - Emits `perf_canary_stage_result{stage="quality_gate_enforcement"}` and per-assertion tags (apply_blocked, promotion_blocked, pass_path_ok).
  - Posts a structured summary to #eng-sre-canaries when failures persist > 2 runs, with links to gate run ids and artifacts.

Behavior / Failure modes:
- Stage is non-flaky by design: retries only on 5xx/timeouts; no retries on assertion failures.
- Hard timeout: 12m for the stage (to avoid blocking the entire canary suite). If evals exceed budget we treat as failure and page as SLO regression (this catches the "gates blocking rollouts too long" class of issues).
- Guardrails: stage aborts early if canary environment is not "staging" or if the route/account id doesn’t match the allowlist.

Rollout:
- Enabled in staging canary pipeline immediately.
- For prod canary pipeline, staged behind `CANARY_ENABLE_QUALITY_GATE_STAGE=true` (default off) so SRE can enable after one week of stable staging signal.

Notes:
- This PR does not change gating logic; it only validates existing behavior and surfaces regressions earlier.
- Related: ENG-3928 (rollout controller respects gate), ENG-3926 (metrics), ENG-3931 (required checks).

Screenshots/Artifacts:
- Example success run output is included in the PR comments (log excerpt + metric names).

title: Add representative workload suite to nightly canary + gating mode for release branches
preview: Context: As part of the Q1 2026 “Perf baseline & representative workload suite” project, we need the representative suite to run continuously (nightly) and also to be usable as a release gate for runtime release branches. This PR wires the representative suite runner into perf-canary with two execution modes: (1) nightly mode (signal + trend) and (2) gating mode (pass/fail) intended for release/* branches. Summary of changes: - Adds a new job definition for the representative suite (chat streaming, long-context prefill, embeddings high-QPS; tool-calling is stubbed in config but not enabled by default yet). - Introduces a `mode` flag: `nightly` vs `gating`. Nightly publishes full metric payloads and trends; gating evaluates a smaller set of canonical metrics against threshold config and emits a single PASS/FAIL with links to artifacts. - Adds threshold configuration files keyed by (model_family, workload, gpu_sku, region) with explicit metric boundaries: TTFT p50/p95/p99, TPOT p50/p95, throughput (tokens/s or req/s for embeddings), plus guardrail saturation signals. - Adds result reporting integration: JSON summary + a markdown report posted to the run output (and to the existing perf-canary comment bot when invoked from a PR workflow). - Adds a “compare-to-baseline” helper that reads the baseline anchor from the run manifest (per ADR-0172) and computes deltas with a simple noise model (min sample size + absolute and relative thresholds). - Adds routing behavior: nightly runs post to #perf-canary (non-paging); gating runs post to #perf-triage (non-paging) and only page on repeated failures (3 consecutive gating failures on the same release branch + same cohort). Important operational notes: - Gating is intentionally fail-closed for release branches (i.e., it will block promotion) but has an explicit override mechanism via a signed config exception (see `docs/gating-exceptions.md`). - We treat missing baseline for a cohort as “SKIP” (non-blocking) to avoid halting releases when a new cohort is added before the baseline sweep catches up. - Cold vs warm cache: gating evaluates both when available; nightly always reports both. By default, gating fails only on cold regressions (warm regressions are warnings) until we finish calibrating cache variance across SKUs. Related: - Linear: ENG-4828 - Confluence: Perf regression policy & thresholds (company standard) - ADR-0172 run manifest schema (consumed by this runner) Testing: - Added unit tests for threshold parsing + delta evaluation. - Ran a dry-run on us-east/H100 with a known-good baseline anchor; verified PASS in gating mode and successful artifact upload. - Simulated a regression by pinning an older baseline + forcing a degraded threshold; verified FAIL output and Slack routing. Rollout plan: - Enable nightly representative suite runs first (starting next scheduled window) and observe variance panels for 1 week. - Enable gating mode for `release/runtime-*` branches after Sana/Sean confirm calibrated thresholds for H100 + A100 cohorts. - Expand to eu-west once baseline sweep completes for that region. Notes / TODO: - Tool/function-calling workload is present in config but not scheduled; will land separately once the generator stabilizes. - Some cohorts are commented out pending L40S baseline completion.

title: Shadow traffic overhead benchmark suite (routing mirror + telemetry) for go/no-go gates
preview:
Context: As part of Shadow Traffic (quality eval metrics) we need a repeatable way to quantify overhead from (a) request mirroring to shadow targets, (b) additional telemetry emission, and (c) backpressure/circuit breaker behavior under scoring/ingestion degradation. This suite is intended to run as a go/no-go gate for beta/GA (and as a nightly regression check).

What this PR does: 1) Adds a benchmark harness to perf-canary that drives realistic inference traffic (chat + streaming chat) through the Redwood gateway with shadowing enabled/disabled and varying sampling rates. 2) Captures user-path latency deltas and resource overhead while ensuring fail-open semantics (shadow path failures must not move user-path SLOs). 3) Emits a standardized results bundle (JSON + markdown) for consumption in rollout readiness docs and for CI gating.

Scenarios included: - Chat (non-streaming): 256 and 1024 token outputs, fixed prompt distribution; measures p50/p95/p99 end-to-end latency, gateway CPU, and request error rates. - Streaming chat: measures time-to-first-token (TTFT), inter-chunk cadence, and tail latency; validates partial-chunk handling when shadow timeout trips. - Shadow sampling rates: 0% (baseline), 1%, 5%, 10%. - Shadow target behaviors: healthy, slow (adds 150ms), erroring (5xx), and unavailable (connection refused). - Telemetry modes: control-only vs control+shadow pair events (cardinality-safe dimensions only).

Go/no-go thresholds (current defaults; tunable via config): - User-path p99 delta <= +3ms at 1% sampling and <= +8ms at 10% sampling (non-streaming), measured at steady state (post-warmup). - Streaming TTFT p99 delta <= +5ms at 1% sampling and <= +12ms at 10% sampling. - Gateway CPU increase <= +5% absolute at 10% sampling under the reference load (see configs). - No statistically significant increase in user-path 5xx/timeout rate when shadow target is failing; circuit breaker should trip and shadow requests should be dropped while user path remains stable.

Outputs: - artifacts/shadow_overhead/<run_id>/results.json (machine-readable) - artifacts/shadow_overhead/<run_id>/summary.md (human-readable) - optional Prometheus snapshot export for deeper debugging

Notes: - This suite intentionally does not validate quality metrics correctness; it only measures performance/overhead and safety behavior. - Test prompts are synthetic and contain no customer data. - The benchmark defaults are aligned with the current routing implementation (mirroring inline with strict timeouts) and the SRE fail-open requirements.

Follow-ups: - Integrate this suite into the release-engineering feature flag plan as a required gate for enabling shadow traffic beyond internal tenants (tracked in ENG-5412). - Add an embeddings workload scenario once the shadow path supports embeddings consistently (out of scope for this PR).

title: Add baseline fetch + regression evaluator (min samples, rolling window, pct thresholds)
preview:
Implements the baseline lookup path and the first pass of regression evaluation logic for perf-canary. This is the missing piece between “we can generate load + emit raw latency/tps metrics” and “we can decide whether something is actually regressing (and do so with low noise)”. The evaluator is intentionally conservative for phase 1: it enforces minimum sample sizes, uses a rolling window over recent canary runs, and compares observed vs baseline using percent-change thresholds per metric.

Context / why: We’ve seen early canary runs spike due to batch composition variance and telemetry ingestion jitter. We need a consistent evaluation step that (a) won’t flap on small sample sizes, (b) is explicit about confidence, and (c) produces a stable verdict we can wire into alert rules + rollout gates.

What’s included:
- Baseline client: fetches baseline artifacts from the Baseline Store service (read-only) using a stable key.
- Baseline keying: model_id + model_version + runtime_build + hardware_sku (+ region when configured). The default is region-agnostic to avoid exploding baseline count; region-specific is opt-in per model via config.
- Regression evaluator:
  - Requires min_samples per metric (default 200 requests per window; configurable).
  - Rolling evaluation window: N most recent intervals (default 10m window with 1m buckets; configurable). Buckets are aggregated using trimmed mean for rate metrics and quantile merge for latency histograms.
  - Thresholds are percent-change relative to baseline, expressed per metric as {warn, page} (page is not used by this repo yet but is emitted in verdict payload for downstream alerting).
  - Anti-flap: uses “M-of-K” rule for verdict state transitions (default 3 of last 5 windows must violate threshold) and a cooldown period before clearing (default 15m) to avoid oscillation.
- Missing/invalid baseline handling: if baseline is absent, stale, or incompatible (e.g., schema mismatch), we emit a “NO_BASELINE” verdict and skip regression evaluation (still emits raw metrics). This is intended to be safe during early rollout.
- Output: emits a structured regression verdict (JSON log event + metrics) containing baseline stats, observed stats, computed deltas, and a confidence score. (Alert webhook payload formatting is handled in a follow-up PR.)

Metric coverage (phase 1):
- e2e_latency_ms (p50/p95/p99)
- runtime_latency_ms (p50/p95/p99) when runtime instrumentation is present
- tokens_per_sec
- queueing_time_ms (if present; treated as attribution signal rather than primary regression trigger)

Notes / non-goals:
- No baseline writing here (owned by baseline generator job / pipeline).
- No customer prompt shadowing. Canary inputs remain synthetic.
- Not attempting “auto-baseline drift correction” in this PR; we only validate staleness via baseline metadata + a max_age guard.

Config additions:
- baseline:
  - endpoint (required)
  - request_timeout_ms
  - max_age_hours (default 168)
  - include_region_in_key (default false; can be overridden per model)
- evaluator:
  - window_minutes, bucket_minutes
  - min_samples
  - violation_m_of_k, violation_k
  - clear_cooldown_minutes
  - thresholds: per-metric warn/page pct deltas

Testing:
- Unit tests for key construction + baseline deserialization.
- Golden tests for evaluator verdicts across common cases: below threshold, above threshold, too few samples, missing baseline, mixed metrics.
- Property-ish test for anti-flap (verdict only flips after M-of-K is satisfied).

Rollout plan:
- Run in observe-only mode for us-east for 2–3 models; validate that verdicts match expectation against staging baselines.
- After 1 week of stability, enable warning-level alert rules in observability-pack.

Screenshots / examples:
- Example verdict log line is included in docs/perf-canary/evaluator.md (shows per-metric deltas + confidence).

Refs:
- Linear: ENG-4874 (baseline store + versioning), ENG-4873 (metric defs)
- Upcoming: pr-19244 (alert webhook payload), observability-pack alert rules PRs

Operational concerns:
- Baseline fetch is cached in-process with a TTL (default 10m) to avoid hammering the store.
- All emitted labels are cardinality-reviewed: model_id, model_version, runtime_build, hardware_sku, region (optional). No request IDs, no prompt hashes.

Requested reviewer focus:
- Nadia/Logan: baseline keying + staleness/compat semantics
- Sofia: rolling window choices + anti-flap logic (do we have enough dampening?)
- Allison: signal naming and whether the output shape is sufficient for downstream alerting/runbooks

title: Add mixed-length burst scenario + thresholds for KV compaction regressions
preview:
Motivation: The KV cache compaction work (runtime-1.22) needs a perf-canary signal that reliably reproduces fragmentation + churn patterns we see in production, and that fails fast when compaction/eviction introduces decode stalls or tail latency spikes. Existing canary scenarios (steady decode, long prompts) don’t create enough allocator churn to surface the regressions we saw in staging.

This PR adds a new scenario (`mixed_length_burst`) that generates mixed prompt lengths + bursty arrivals + cancellations, and wires pass/fail thresholds + alert routing that are specific to compaction regressions.

What’s included: - New canary scenario: mixed-length burst (configurable length distribution + burst profile) - KPI collection updates: capture p95/p99 latency, TTFT, throughput, and the runtime-exposed compaction/eviction signals when present (bytes moved / compaction time / eviction rate) - Thresholds + gating: scenario-specific failure criteria (tail latency + compaction correlation guardrails) - Alert routing: send failures to `#eng-sre` + `#eng-runtime` with distinct annotation so oncall can quickly identify compaction-related failures vs baseline infra noise - Docs: scenario description + how to interpret failures + links to dashboards

Notes/constraints: - This PR does not require compaction to be enabled; it will still run against baseline runtime versions. When compaction metrics are absent, the scenario gates on latency/throughput only and emits a warning annotation rather than failing. - Thresholds were calibrated using recent benchmark-lab results and staging canary runs; see “Calibration” below.

Calibration summary: - Target workload: mixed prefill lengths (p10~64 tok, p50~256 tok, p90~1.5k tok), decode lengths with a long tail, 20–40% burst uplift for 90s windows, 5–10% cancellation rate. - Failure criteria (initial): (a) p99 decode latency regression > 12% vs control build OR absolute p99 exceeds scenario SLO cap; (b) compaction_bytes_moved_per_s sustained > budget + correlated p99 spike; (c) eviction_rate sustained above threshold AND output token rate drops below floor.

Rollout plan: - Land scenario behind `--enable-scenario=mixed_length_burst` flag for one week - Enable in nightly canary for Hosted GPU pools first; then expand to Dedicated canary cohorts once we have stable baselines

Related: - ENG-4828 (scenario) - runtime PRs: compaction metrics/tracing are landing separately in the monorepo; this PR only consumes exported telemetry when available.

title: perf-canary: add QPS rate limits + per-region capacity budget (GPU overhead guardrail)
preview:
Context: As we move perf-canary from staging into a production region, we need hard guardrails so the canary cannot consume meaningful GPU capacity or accidentally fan out due to config mistakes. This PR implements (1) explicit QPS caps with a token-bucket limiter, and (2) a per-region capacity budget that converts an overhead target (e.g., 0.5% of region GPU capacity) into an allowed request rate/concurrency for the canary. The goal is: safe by default, predictable overhead, and an immediate kill-switch when budgets are exceeded.

What this adds (high-level): - Rate limiting: token bucket limiter at the request scheduler layer (covers both steady-state QPS and burst). - Capacity budget: `capacity_budget` config per region (defaults to 0.003 = 0.3% until we tune) with optional overrides per tier/model group. - Budget accounting: estimates GPU-seconds/request using (a) last known baseline runtime-only latency for the model+SKU, falling back to (b) a conservative static estimate when baseline is missing. - Concurrency guardrail: hard cap on in-flight requests derived from budget; limiter blocks and emits a "budget exceeded" metric instead of pushing load. - Safety valves: (a) global max_qps, (b) global max_in_flight, (c) "safe_disable" config flag to stop generating traffic while still emitting health metrics. - Telemetry: new counters/histograms so SRE can confirm overhead stays under budget and catch misconfig quickly.

Operational intent: The canary should stay <0.5% of GPU capacity per region for covered models (goal in project doc). We are starting with tighter defaults (0.3% budget, max 0.6 rps per model group, max 4 in-flight) and will loosen only after we see stable overhead in us-east rollout.

Notes/assumptions: - We treat the canary as "worst case" for overhead: no reliance on customer KV cache, no warm batching assumptions. - If baseline is stale or missing, we err toward limiting harder (pessimistic runtime estimate). - This PR does not change the prompt distribution; it just changes how quickly we run it.

Follow-ups: - Integrate budget inputs with control-plane config surface once PR-19203 lands in `redwood` (config validation + audit log). - Add per-SKU budget overrides once we have eu-west A10 vs H100 variance characterized (ENG-4882).

title: Add synthetic canary for graceful degradation path (model/region fallback)
preview: Summary: This adds a new synthetic canary test that exercises Redwood’s graceful degradation behavior (fallback model and/or fallback region) and emits low-cardinality signals that map directly to incident taxonomy categories (Routing/Fallback misbehavior, Capacity/Fleet shortage, Control plane/routing config). Motivation: Recent incidents and taxonomy workshops highlighted that we don’t currently have a reliable, continuous signal that the “degrade gracefully” path is actually working end-to-end (router decision -> fallback selection -> request succeeds -> correct fallback_reason emitted). This canary is intentionally not a throughput/perf benchmark; it is a correctness + reliability smoke test with a small number of requests on a fixed cadence. What it does: (1) Sends a small chat completion request using a policy that prefers a primary model/region, but is allowed to fall back to a designated compatible model and a designated secondary region. (2) Validates that the response is successful and that the response metadata includes a structured fallback_reason when a fallback occurs (or explicitly indicates no fallback when not needed). (3) Publishes Prometheus metrics and structured logs that can be used in incident tooling/dashboards without exploding label cardinality. Key signals emitted: - perf_canary_degradation_success{tier="hosted",path="model"|"region"|"none"} - perf_canary_degradation_latency_ms_bucket{tier="hosted",path=...} - perf_canary_degradation_fallback_reason{tier="hosted",reason="capacity"|"health"|"policy"|"timeout"|"unknown"} (allowlist only; unknown is a single bucket) - perf_canary_degradation_expected_fallback_mismatch_total{tier="hosted",expected="model"|"region",observed=...} Mapping notes (incident taxonomy): - Sustained failures in this canary are an early warning for Routing/Fallback misbehavior vs Capacity shortage. The metric split (reason allowlist) is aligned with the router fallback_reason enums added in redwood, and is intended to support ENG-4824 taxonomy instrumentation. Rollout/ops: - Default: enabled in prod canary clusters at 1 req/min per region pair, small payload (<= 128 output tokens). - Alerting thresholds are intentionally conservative (multi-window, minimum burn) to avoid paging on transient blips; see follow-up in observability-pack. - Credentials: uses existing perf-canary service account and a dedicated routing policy ID restricted to canary traffic. Testing: - Unit tests for metadata parsing + allowlist mapping. - Integration test runs in CI against mock router endpoint. Notes: No customer-facing change. This is internal telemetry only.

title: traffic burst shielding and graceful-degradation rollout guard
preview:
Motivation: we see a class of incidents where sudden client-driven traffic bursts (spikes in request ingress slope) overwhelm KV-cache memory and batching heuristics, producing high tail latency and queueing even when nominal throughput is within capacity. Existing canary detectors focus on gradual regressions or static threshold crossings; they tend to either miss very short sharp bursts or trigger hard rollbacks that are disruptive for live rollouts.

This PR introduces 'Burst Shielding' — a new detector + graded mitigation rollout-guard that: 1) detects sudden traffic bursts using a fused signal of p99 latency delta, request ingress slope, request queue depth, and per-device KV cache eviction rate; 2) classifies burst severity with a Nyquist-inspired adaptive smoothing and causal-change detector to avoid false positives on regular traffic ramps; 3) applies graded, time-bound mitigations that prefer graceful degradation over hard rollback: scale temporary concurrency tokens, disable batching for affected routes, route a percentage of traffic to a low-latency variant (quantized/FP16), and raise an alert with suggested manual actions.

What this change does (high level):
- Adds a new detector implementation: perf_canary/detectors/traffic_burst_detector.py that computes a fused burst score and severity banding. Uses EWMA slope estimation + causal change point test to detect sub-minute bursts.
- Adds an orchestration policy: perf_canary/policies/burst_shield_policy.py that defines mitigation steps per severity and a cooldown window.
- Adds config and rollout guard integration: deployments/canary/burst_shield.yaml (tunable knobs: slope_window, score_thresholds, mitigation_sequence).
- Adds telemetry: metrics/telemetry.md and exporters for burst_score, kv_eviction_rate, ingress_slope, burst_mitigation_state.
- Adds integration tests and an end-to-end harness: tests/integration/test_burst_shield.py that simulates traffic bursts at varying ramp rates.
- Adds runbook and docs: docs/runbooks/burst-shield.md and a short guide in docs/canary-patterns.md.
- Small runtime changes: a light-weight KV eviction metric hook in serving/runtime/kv_stats.c and a debounced batching toggle in serving/runtime/batching_controller.go.

Checklist: 
- [x] Detector implementation and unit tests
- [x] Policy orchestration and integration tests
- [x] CI flakiness investigation (initial failing e2e)
- [x] Docs and runbook written
- [x] Linear items linked

Safety and rationale for graded mitigation: instead of immediately rejecting the rollout, Burst Shielding prefers ephemeral, reversible actions that reduce tail latency without discarding a rollout (e.g., disabling batching for the affected route reduces queuing at the cost of per-token CPU, which is acceptable for short-lived bursts). Routing to a low-latency variant is limited by percentage and time to reduce correctness risk.

Backward compatibility: default policy is off for existing canaries; enabling is opt-in via the manifest. No public API surface is changed.

References: ENG-7842 (detection spec), PM-221 (customer incident summary), internal perf notes (link omitted).

title: Add region + hardware SKU dimension config (with fanout safeguards)
preview:
## Summary This PR adds first-class configuration for running perf-canary across multiple **regions** and **hardware SKUs**, while ensuring we don’t accidentally fan out to every region/SKU combination (and burn the overhead budget). The canary runner now supports: (1) Target selection by `region` and `hardware_sku` (optional), (2) A hard fanout guard that defaults to conservative behavior, and (3) Safer defaults for Helm/Env-based deployments. This is needed for Phase 1 rollout where we want to start in `us-east` on a single SKU, then expand gradually to `eu-west` and additional SKUs once baselines/thresholds are tuned.

## Motivation / Context Today the canary targets are effectively “global” (implicitly whatever the control plane routes to). That makes it hard to: - Attribute regressions to fleet changes (SKU-specific) vs runtime changes - Set region-specific baselines (telemetry variance differs per region) - Control cost/overhead during rollout (avoid accidental wide fanout)

This change is intentionally scoped to **config + selection + safety rails**. Baselines/threshold wiring is handled by baseline fetch logic in a separate PR.

## Design - **Target dimensions:** `region`, `hardware_sku` are added as *optional* dimensions to the canary target config. If `hardware_sku` is omitted, the canary runs “per region” and relies on the inference tier routing for SKU; if provided, we force requests to that SKU via the internal routing hint header (same mechanism used by benchmark-lab).
- **Fanout safeguards:** - New config key: `fanout_max_targets` (default: `3`) - New config key: `fanout_allowlist` (optional explicit list of `<region>:<sku>` tuples). If set, only those tuples run. - New startup-time safety check: if computed target count exceeds `fanout_max_targets`, the process exits non-zero unless `--allow-fanout-over-max` is explicitly set (intended only for controlled manual runs). - New “blast radius” log line on startup prints the exact computed targets and the derived capacity budget per target.
- **Metric labels / cardinality:** Region is now a standard label on canary-emitted metrics. Hardware SKU is only emitted when explicitly configured (to avoid accidental cardinality growth); when omitted, we emit `hardware_sku="unknown"` for aggregator stability.
- **Alert payload:** Alert webhook payload includes `region` + `hardware_sku` in the `scope` section so incident-bot threads are dedupe-able per tuple.

## Config changes - New config schema fields (YAML): - `targets.regions` (list) - `targets.hardware_skus` (list, optional) - `targets.matrix_mode` (one of: `region_only`, `region_x_sku`) - `safety.fanout_max_targets` (int) - `safety.fanout_allowlist` (list of strings `region:sku` or `region:*`) - `safety.require_explicit_region` (bool, default true)

Example (Phase 1): ```yaml targets:   regions: ["us-east-1"]   matrix_mode: "region_only" safety:   fanout_max_targets: 2   require_explicit_region: true ```

Example (Phase 2 w/ SKU): ```yaml targets:   regions: ["us-east-1", "eu-west-1"]   hardware_skus: ["h100-sxm", "a100-80gb"]   matrix_mode: "region_x_sku" safety:   fanout_allowlist: ["us-east-1:h100-sxm", "eu-west-1:h100-sxm"]   fanout_max_targets: 3 ```

## Implementation notes - Added `TargetMatrix` helper to compute the final run list. This keeps selection logic out of the request loop and makes it unit-testable. - Added request routing hints: `X-Redwood-Region` and `X-Redwood-Hardware-Sku` (only when configured). These are already supported server-side for internal callers; the perf-canary service account is restricted to internal endpoints. - Extended the runner loop to include `target_scope` in logs and metric tags.

## Tests - Unit tests for matrix expansion, allowlist filtering, max-target guard, and stable ordering (so rollouts are deterministic). - Updated config loader tests to validate schema errors (e.g., `region_x_sku` with missing `hardware_skus`). - Added a small “dry-run” mode test that prints computed targets and exits.

## Rollout plan - Merge behind conservative defaults: `require_explicit_region=true`, `fanout_max_targets=3`, `matrix_mode=region_only`. - Phase 1 (ENG-4880): enable `us-east-1` only, no SKU dimension. - Phase 2: introduce SKU dimension for `h100-sxm` only, validate baseline alignment, then expand.

## Risks / Mitigations - Risk: accidental fanout increases load/cost. Mitigation: hard guard + allowlist option + explicit override flag. - Risk: metrics cardinality blowup. Mitigation: hardware_sku label only when explicitly configured; region label is bounded and already present in most dashboards.

## Screenshots / Output (dry-run) - `--dry-run` output example: `computed_targets=[us-east-1:*] target_count=1 fanout_max_targets=3` - `region_x_sku` example: `computed_targets=[us-east-1:h100-sxm, eu-west-1:h100-sxm] target_count=2 fanout_allowlist=2`

## Follow-ups - Baseline store needs to key off (model, runtime_version, sku, region) once we enable SKU dimension broadly. - Confirm incident-bot dedupe keys include region+sku (separate PR in incident-bot repo).

title: Add log redaction + no-PII assertions for perf-canary request/telemetry path
preview: Motivation: perf-canary must never emit or persist customer data. While the canary uses synthetic prompts, we still had a few sharp edges where (a) debug logging could print request bodies on errors and (b) some error/reporting paths would include raw prompt text / user-provided identifiers if they ever slipped through config. This PR adds explicit redaction + hard assertions so we fail-closed and keep logs/metrics safe. Summary of changes: (1) Introduce a centralized redaction helper for log fields and error payloads; (2) Add runtime assertions that enforce "synthetic-only" prompt sources (no external prompt injection), with an opt-out only for local dev; (3) Ensure we never log request bodies, prompt strings, or headers that may contain identifiers; (4) Add "safe fields" allowlist for alert webhook payloads so incident-bot never receives prompt content; (5) Add tests that simulate error paths (HTTP 4xx/5xx, timeouts, JSON decode errors) and verify log output + alert payload are redacted; (6) Add lightweight "no_pii" static checks on structured logging calls in canary request loop. Operational notes: if assertions trip in prod, the canary request will be dropped and counted under canary_safety_dropped_requests_total with reason=no_pii_guard; the process stays healthy and continues other model targets. Checklist: [x] No prompt strings in logs on success/error paths [x] No customer identifiers in metrics labels [x] Alert payload contains only model/region/version/baseline/observed deltas + links [x] Unit tests for redaction + guardrails [x] Docs note in README about dev-only override. Risk: low/medium; touches error handling and logging. Rollout: safe to deploy alongside existing canary; no config change required. Follow-ups: add a prometheus metric for redaction_invocations_total to track unexpected surfaces; consider moving dev-only override behind a build tag.
