title: perf-canary: add standardized alert webhook payload format for incident-bot
preview:
Context: incident-bot ingestion currently relies on parsing semi-structured strings emitted by perf-canary. This has made dedupe noisy and makes it hard to attach the right dashboard/runbook links (esp. when we start running canary per region + SKU). This PR introduces a versioned, stable webhook payload schema (v1) and a small formatter that produces consistent alert payloads across warning/page severities.

What this changes: Adds a `WebhookPayloadV1` struct + builder with a single entrypoint `FormatAlertPayload(ctx, evalResult, linkSet)`. The payload includes the primary identifiers (model, model_version, region, tier, hardware_sku), regression metrics (observed vs baseline, deltas, thresholds), confidence/sample sizing details, and a normalized set of links (Grafana dashboard, logs query, trace search, runbook). The incident-bot handler will use these fields for dedupe and rendering.

Design notes: (1) Versioning: payload includes `schema_version: "v1"` and is forwards-compatible (new fields can be added without breaking old consumers). (2) Cardinality guardrails: the payload includes *identifiers* needed for routing and triage, but explicitly excludes request-level IDs and anything derived from prompts. Kernel/build fields are constrained to a small, stable set (build_id, kernel_selection_id) that are already present in runtime telemetry; no raw compiler flags or per-host info. (3) Deterministic dedupe: payload includes `dedupe_key` computed from {schema_version, alert_type, severity, model, model_version, region, tier, hardware_sku, runtime_build_id, kernel_selection_id}. This is intended to align with incident-bot’s thread grouping behavior.

Payload schema (v1) at a glance (example): {"schema_version":"v1","alert_type":"perf_regression","severity":"warning","dedupe_key":"v1|perf_regression|warning|llama-3.1-70b-instruct|2026-01-14|us-east|hosted|h100-sxm|runtime-2026.01.15.3|kernel-attn-v7","occurred_at":"2026-01-16T22:14:05Z","target":{"model":"llama-3.1-70b-instruct","model_version":"2026-01-14","region":"us-east","tier":"hosted","hardware_sku":"h100-sxm","runtime_build_id":"runtime-2026.01.15.3","kernel_selection_id":"kernel-attn-v7"},"regression":{"metric":"e2e_latency_p95_ms","baseline":{"value":238.1,"window":"baseline:runtime-2026.01.10.1/h100-sxm (7d)","p50":201.4,"p95":238.1,"p99":310.2},"observed":{"value":291.9,"window":"observed:15m","p50":238.8,"p95":291.9,"p99":402.7},"delta":{"absolute":53.8,"percent":22.6},"threshold":{"percent":15.0,"min_samples":200,"min_window":"10m"},"samples":{"request_count":482,"token_count":185440,"unique_shapes":12},"confidence":{"score":0.86,"method":"bootstrap_ci","p_value":0.004}},"attribution":{"prefill_latency_p95_ms":{ "baseline":91.2, "observed":104.5, "delta_percent":14.6 },"decode_latency_p95_ms":{ "baseline":120.4, "observed":160.8, "delta_percent":33.5 },"queue_time_p95_ms":{ "baseline":9.1, "observed":10.2, "delta_percent":12.1 },"tokens_per_sec":{ "baseline":182.0, "observed":151.6, "delta_percent":-16.7 },"batch_size_p50":{ "baseline":3.0, "observed":3.0, "delta_percent":0.0 }},"links":{"dashboard":"https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/perf-canary/perf-canary?var-model=llama-3.1-70b-instruct&var-region=us-east&var-sku=h100-sxm","logs":"https://logs.redwood.ai/search?query=service%3Dperf-canary+model%3Dllama-3.1-70b-instruct+region%3Dus-east","traces":"https://tracing.redwood.ai/search?service=perf-canary&model=llama-3.1-70b-instruct&region=us-east","runbook":"https://confluence.redwood.ai/x/runbook-perf-regression-canary"},"annotations":{"release_candidate":"runtime-2026.01.15.3","rollout_stage":"phase-1"}}

Files/areas changed: New payload types and formatter in perf-canary alerting package; webhook sender now posts JSON with Content-Type: application/json and includes schema_version; Adds unit tests for (a) stable dedupe key generation, (b) percent delta formatting and rounding behavior, (c) link generation and required link presence by severity, (d) redaction assertions (no prompt text fields, no request IDs); Updates docs in repo for incident-bot integration expectations.

Compatibility: Existing string-based payload is still emitted in logs, but the webhook is now the structured JSON payload. Incident-bot can safely ignore unknown fields. If incident-bot isn’t updated yet, the webhook receiver will still see the message, but formatting may be degraded; see rollout notes below.

Rollout plan: (1) Merge this PR behind `PERF_CANARY_ALERT_PAYLOAD_SCHEMA=v1` (default on in staging, default off in prod). (2) Coordinate with incident-bot handler update (repo: incident-bot) to switch renderer to v1 (Rafael/Allison). (3) Flip prod flag per region after verifying dashboard/runbook links. (4) Remove legacy formatting once canary paging is enabled phase-1.

Checklist: - [x] Schema versioned (v1) - [x] Dedupe key stable and documented - [x] No prompt/customer identifiers included - [x] Unit tests for formatter and sender - [x] Links validated in us-east staging - [x] Linear linked (ENG-4878)

Refs: Linear ENG-4878 (alerting policy + routing), ENG-4872 (MVP perf canary). Related PRs: observability-pack PR for alert rules (warning/page), incident-bot PR for handler support (upcoming).

title: Add synthetic prompt generator + prompt-shape buckets (seedable workload profiles)
preview: Summary: This PR adds a synthetic prompt-shape generator and a seedable workload profile so perf-canary runs are reproducible across regions/hosts and can be tuned without touching code. The goal is to approximate production request shapes (prefill/decode mix, prompt length buckets) without using any customer prompts or identifiers. Why: Baselines and regression detection require stable distributions. Early canary scaffolding used a fixed prompt and random lengths, which made p95 latency and tokens/sec variance too high and made comparisons across runtime versions noisy. What’s included: 1) PromptShape + WorkloadProfile config types (YAML) with explicit length buckets and weights; 2) Seeded RNG for selecting shapes, generation params, and per-run determinism; 3) A small library that produces “prompt skeletons” (shape only) that can be filled by a static, non-sensitive text corpus shipped with the service; 4) Guardrails to prevent accidental generation of unique/high-cardinality prompts (bounded vocabulary + truncation + stable templates). Non-goals: This does not attempt to match semantic content, tool-calling schemas, or customer-specific formatting. This is intentionally shape-only for privacy and stability. Follow-ups: Add a control-plane surface for selecting profiles per model/region, and tune weights based on aggregated production histograms (without collecting prompts).

title: Emit metrics for end-to-end latency and tokens/sec (per-model) with cardinality guardrails
preview: Summary: This PR adds first-pass metric emission for perf-canary runs: end-to-end latency histograms and tokens/sec (plus supporting counters) with per-model attribution. Metrics are emitted from the canary process (not the runtime) so we can alert even when runtime telemetry pipelines are delayed. Motivation: We need low-noise, per-model signals for p50/p95/p99 latency and throughput (tokens/sec) before we can wire dashboards/alerting. This also sets the label policy up front to avoid a repeat of past cardinality blowups (e.g., per-prompt identifiers or unconstrained model strings). Metrics added (Prometheus/OpenTelemetry bridge): perf_canary_request_e2e_latency_ms (histogram; attributes: model_id, model_version, region, tier, hardware_sku, outcome); perf_canary_request_tokens_per_second (histogram; computed from generated_tokens / decode_time; same attributes); perf_canary_generated_tokens_total (counter; same attributes); perf_canary_requests_total (counter; same attributes); perf_canary_metric_dropped_total (counter; attributes: metric_name, drop_reason) to make guardrail drops visible. Cardinality guardrails: model_id + model_version are sourced from control-plane config and validated against an allowlist at startup; we reject/replace unknown model identifiers with model_id="unknown" and increment perf_canary_metric_dropped_total (drop_reason=unknown_model). We also hard-cap unique label sets per process (default 500) to fail closed (drop + log) if someone accidentally adds a high-card dimension. Explicitly not emitted: prompt text, prompt hashes, request ids, customer ids, raw error strings. outcome is normalized to a small enum (ok | timeout | http_error | inference_error | cancelled). Notes: tokens/sec is computed as generated_tokens / max(decode_wall_ms, 1ms) and emitted only for successful runs; for failures we still emit latency + counters to avoid survivorship bias in alerting. Follow-ups: This PR does not include baseline comparison logic (see PR-19218) or region/SKU fanout config (PR-19226).

Metrics added (Prometheus/OpenTelemetry bridge): perf_canary_request_e2e_latency_ms (histogram; attributes: model_id, model_version, region, tier, hardware_sku, outcome); perf_canary_request_tokens_per_second (histogram; computed from generated_tokens / decode_time; same attributes); perf_canary_generated_tokens_total (counter; same attributes); perf_canary_requests_total (counter; same attributes); perf_canary_metric_dropped_total (counter; attributes: metric_name, drop_reason) to make guardrail drops visible. Cardinality guardrails: model_id + model_version are sourced from control-plane config and validated against an allowlist at startup; we reject/replace unknown model identifiers with model_id="unknown" and increment perf_canary_metric_dropped_total (drop_reason=unknown_model). We also hard-cap unique label sets per process (default 500) to fail closed (drop + log) if someone accidentally adds a high-card dimension. Explicitly not emitted: prompt text, prompt hashes, request ids, customer ids, raw error strings. outcome is normalized to a small enum (ok | timeout | http_error | inference_error | cancelled). Notes: tokens/sec is computed as generated_tokens / max(decode_wall_ms, 1ms) and emitted only for successful runs; for failures we still emit latency + counters to avoid survivorship bias in alerting. Follow-ups: This PR does not include baseline comparison logic (see PR-19218) or region/SKU fanout config (PR-19226).

title: perf-canary: add optimization config safeguards + gate signal publisher for latency regressions
preview:
Title: perf-canary: add optimization config safeguards + gate signal publisher for latency regressions

Context
Safe apply + rollback for Optimization Configs needs an external, repeatable “canary judge” that can detect config-induced latency regressions early and produce machine-readable gate signals. Today, on-call relies on dashboards + manual judgement, which slows progression and increases risk (see internal incident notes around p95 spikes after caching/batching tweaks).

What this PR does
- Adds a new scheduled/on-demand canary workflow ("optimization-safeguard") that:
  - Detects active Optimization Config rollout scopes (tenant/model/region + canary %) and config_version.
  - Pulls baseline vs canary metrics (p95/p99 latency + 5xx rate) for the last N minutes, with warmup handling.
  - Evaluates against configurable thresholds with sane defaults (latency regression % + absolute ms guard, and error-rate delta).
  - Publishes gate signals to the control-plane gate ingest endpoint so the rollout controller can pause/fail a stage.
- Standardizes metric tagging requirements used by the gates: tenant_id, model, region, route, config_version, stage_id.
- Produces a clear human-readable summary in the job logs for on-call (what scope, what changed, baseline vs canary values, and decision).

Design notes / decisions
- Windowing: uses a rolling 10m evaluation window with a 3m warmup skip after stage start to reduce false positives from cache churn. Stage start time is fetched from the rollout controller (if unavailable, falls back to “first seen” timestamp for config_version).
- Baseline selection: baseline is “same tenant/model/region, previous stable config_version” (prefer stage.previous_version; else the most recent version marked stable in history). Baseline comparisons require minimum sample sizes (req_count >= 2,000 or token_count >= 3,000,000) to avoid noisy results.
- Threshold defaults (overridable per environment):
  - p95: fail if +12% and +20ms (both) vs baseline
  - p99: fail if +15% and +35ms (both) vs baseline
  - 5xx: fail if +0.15% absolute vs baseline or > 0.50% absolute
- Publishing semantics: the job emits a single gate evaluation per (stage_id, scope) and is idempotent; repeated runs overwrite the same key to avoid “gate spam”.

Implementation overview
- New job runner: cmd/optimization_safeguard_canary (Go) with a single entrypoint supporting both cron + manual trigger.
- New query package: internal/metrics/queries/optimization_rollout.go
  - Uses PromQL (for prod) and a local stub backend for unit tests.
  - Explicitly filters by config_version for canary and baseline.
- New gate publisher: internal/gates/publisher.go
  - Sends GateSignal payloads to /internal/gates/v1/signals (auth via service token + request id header).
  - Payload includes: decision (pass/fail/insufficient_data), reason, observed metrics, baseline metrics, window, and tags.
- Configuration: adds OPTIMIZATION_SAFEGUARD_* env vars (window, warmup, thresholds, min sample sizes, dry-run toggle).

Illustrative payload (log excerpt)
GateSignal{ gate="optimization.latency_regression", stage_id="stg_01J...", scope="tenant=t_8d3 model=llama3.1-70b region=us-east-1", config_version="ocv_1742", decision="fail", reason="p95 +18.4% (+42ms) vs baseline ocv_1739", metrics="p95=270ms p99=690ms 5xx=0.22%", baseline="p95=228ms p99=610ms 5xx=0.07%", window="10m", warmup="3m" }

Files / areas changed (high level)
- cmd/optimization_safeguard_canary/main.go (new)
- internal/metrics/client.go (adds query timeout + request-scoped logger)
- internal/metrics/queries/optimization_rollout.go (new)
- internal/gates/publisher.go (new)
- internal/gates/types.go (adds GateSignalDecision=insufficient_data)
- deploy/cronjobs/optimization-safeguard.yaml (new)
- config/env.sample (new vars + docs)
- tests: internal/metrics/queries/optimization_rollout_test.go, internal/gates/publisher_test.go

Why perf-canary (vs control plane)
We want this logic to be operationally owned by SRE/Obs and to share the same query/patterns as other perf canaries. Control-plane gating stays lightweight: it consumes standardized gate signals.

Testing
- Unit tests for:
  - baseline selection (previous stable vs fallback)
  - warmup skip behavior
  - min sample size -> insufficient_data
  - regression decision logic and threshold overrides
  - publisher idempotency keying
- Local smoke test:
  - DRY_RUN=1 OPTIMIZATION_SAFEGUARD_TARGET=staging
  - Verified log output and query shapes against staging Prometheus.

Operational notes
- Default is “dry-run” in staging and “publish enabled” behind a feature flag in prod (PERF_CANARY_OPTIMIZATION_SAFEGUARD_ENABLED).
- Alerts: not added here; this job is intended to feed rollout gating. Follow-up PR will add an alert on repeated insufficient_data and on publisher errors.

Rollout plan
1) Merge + deploy to staging with dry-run; validate false positive rate during internal beta rollouts.
2) Enable publishing for internal accounts only (feature flag + allowlist of tenant IDs).
3) Expand to limited GA accounts.

Review conversation (highlights)
- Sean Gallagher: “We need explicit handling for cache invalidation warmup; otherwise this will page us all week.”
  - Aisha Bello: Added warmup skip + minimum sample size requirement, and included ‘insufficient_data’ decision so the controller can pause rather than fail.
- Allison Grant: “Please ensure metrics are tagged with config_version everywhere; otherwise the baseline/canary split is brittle.”
  - Aisha Bello: Added an explicit guard that errors if config_version label is missing from query results and logs a structured warning with the rollout scope.
- Benji Okafor: “Gate ingest endpoint requires idempotency; we can’t store an unbounded stream.”
  - Aisha Bello: Implemented deterministic idempotency_key = sha256(stage_id + tenant + model + region + gate_name).
- Sofia Ivanova: “Latency % alone is tricky at low baselines; add absolute ms bounds.”
  - Aisha Bello: Updated thresholds to require both relative and absolute deltas.

CI
- build: pass
- lint: pass
- unit tests: pass
- docker image build: pass

Merge
Merged via squash into main.

Follow-ups
- Add a small library wrapper so control-plane and perf-canary share the same gate rule names/strings.
- Add explicit alerting on publisher errors and ‘insufficient_data’ rates by region.
- Add a “partial rollout” mode to compare canary shards vs non-canary shards within the same config_version when baseline is unavailable.

title: Bootstrap perf-canary service skeleton (config + request loop)
preview:
Summary: This PR bootstraps the initial perf-canary service skeleton: repo layout, config parsing/validation, and a basic request loop that can hit internal inference endpoints on a schedule. This is intentionally minimal (no metrics emission yet, no baseline evaluation yet) so we can land the scaffolding early and iterate behind feature flags.

Context: We need a lightweight, always-on canary workload in prod to catch serving-runtime regressions (p95 latency + tokens/sec) before broad customer impact. This PR is the first building block: a small service that can execute synthetic requests against a curated model list and report per-run status via logs. Follow-ups will add: (1) synthetic prompt generation (shape-only; no customer prompts), (2) structured metrics + histograms, (3) baseline fetch + regression evaluation, and (4) rate limiting/capacity budgeting.

What’s in this PR:
- New Go service under services/perf-canary/ with a single binary (cmd/perf-canary).
- Config layout and validation:
  - YAML config file + env overrides (PERF_CANARY_*).
  - Target definitions: region, endpoint, model, request type.
  - Schedule: interval + jitter.
  - Safety defaults: low QPS (1 worker), strict timeouts, and a hard max on concurrent in-flight requests.
- Basic request loop:
  - Iterates over configured targets and issues a request using an internal service token (provided via env; token wiring done elsewhere).
  - Captures timing spans (wall clock end-to-end) and logs: target, model, status code, elapsed_ms.
  - Does NOT log request/response bodies.
- Minimal client implementation:
  - HTTP client with per-request timeout.
  - Retry policy: 1 retry on 5xx / transient network errors (with bounded backoff).
- Unit tests for config parsing and validation; a small “smoke” integration test that runs against a local stub server.
- README with local dev instructions and example config.

Non-goals (explicitly not in this PR):
- No metrics emission (Prometheus/OTel) yet.
- No baseline storage/comparison.
- No prompt generator (requests use a placeholder short synthetic prompt string; follow-up PR will replace with shape generator + seed control).
- No k8s/Helm/Terraform deployment artifacts.

Operational/Safety notes:
- The service currently logs only metadata (model/target + timings). No prompt text, user identifiers, or response content.
- Config includes a “dry_run” mode that only validates config and prints the planned schedule/targets.

Testing:
- go test ./... (unit + stubbed integration)
- gofmt/goimports

Rollout plan:
- Merge behind a disabled-by-default config. We’ll wire deployment + service account in subsequent PRs and start in a single region with 2–3 models.

Related:
- ENG-4872 (Perf canary prod MVP)
- ENG-4873 (Define per-model regression metrics; upcoming)

Screenshots/log examples (from stub run):
- INFO run completed target=us-east-internal model=llama-3.1-8b status=200 elapsed_ms=312
- WARN request failed target=us-east-internal model=llama-3.1-8b status=503 elapsed_ms=1001 retry=1

title: Add perf-canary gates for hardware profile rollouts (latency + throughput) and adjust alert routing
preview:
Context: As part of the hardware tuning profiles pack rollout (H100/L40S first, B200 gated), we need perf-canary to catch regressions that only show up when `hardware_profile_pack` is enabled and the runtime selects SKU-specific presets (scheduler/kernels/KV-cache budgets). Existing canary jobs compare runtime builds against a baseline, but they don’t segment by “profile-enabled” vs “profile-disabled”, and alerts are currently too noisy (especially on L40S runners per INT-8832). This PR adds explicit gates for profile-enabled builds, tightens/loosens thresholds where appropriate, and updates alert routing so actionable regressions page the right rotation.

What’s in this PR: (1) New gate group `hardware_profiles_rollout` that runs paired experiments (A/B) with `RUNTIME_ENABLE_HARDWARE_PROFILE_PACK=1` (treatment) vs `=0` (control) for the same build SHA where possible, and evaluates deltas relative to the control; (2) Separate thresholds per GPU SKU (H100 vs L40S) and per workload class (short context, long context, streaming) using the workload matrix runner outputs; (3) Guardrails for tail latency: p95 and p99 decode latency deltas are gated more strictly than p50, and we add an explicit gate on “prefill/decode fairness regression” proxy metrics to catch the class of issues we saw in early H100 validation; (4) Throughput gates: tokens/sec must not regress beyond allowed deltas per workload bucket; (5) Reduced-noise evaluation: bootstrap confidence intervals + minimum sample size, and an “unstable runner” annotation path to avoid failing the PR on known infra flake while still emitting a warning; (6) Alert routing: hardware-profile gate failures route to `#eng-runtime` + `#eng-sre` Slack, and only page `runtime-perf-oncall` when (a) p95/p99 latency gate fails or (b) throughput regression exceeds the “page threshold” for two consecutive intervals.

Thresholds (initial defaults): H100: p50 latency delta <= +3%, p95 <= +5% (streaming: +6%), tokens/sec delta >= -2% (short/medium) and >= -3% (long context). L40S: p50 <= +4%, p95 <= +7% (streaming: +8%), tokens/sec delta >= -3% (short/medium) and >= -5% (long context). Note: L40S long-context has higher allowed variance while we address runner stability (INT-8832) and KV-cache fragmentation variance. B200: gates added but are warning-only unless `ENABLE_EXPERIMENTAL_B200_PROFILE_GATES=1` is set (this matches the experimental gating strategy for early B200 bring-up).

Operator notes: The alert payload now includes `profile_pack_version`, `selected_profile_name` (if available), SKU, workload bucket, and the exact threshold that triggered. This is intended to speed up triage during staged pool/region rollouts.

Test plan: Updated unit tests for gate evaluation logic; ran `./canary run hardware_profiles_rollout --dry-run` against last 7 days of artifacts; validated alert routing changes in staging Alertmanager; re-ran on L40S runners to confirm we no longer hard-fail on infra flake without a confidence interval.

Rollout: Merge + monitor canary dashboards for 24h; if false positive rate is still high on L40S, we’ll adjust sample sizes and/or widen only the long-context p95 gate while keeping H100 gates strict.

title: add-load-diffusion-prober-and-smooth-fallback-oracle
preview: Motivation: production rollouts occasionally experienced sharp, short-duration latency spikes when traffic shifts concentrated on a small set of worker shards. This PR introduces a traffic-aware Load Diffusion Prober and a Smooth Fallback Oracle to reduce false-positive rollbacks and provide graceful stall protection during bursty traffic. The Load Diffusion Prober continuously samples routing keys and estimates effective shard utilization and collision probability; when hotspots are detected it adjusts probe weights and issues soft-penalties instead of hard rollbacks. The Smooth Fallback Oracle consumes prober signals plus percentile-residual smoothing to decide fallback windows and maximum rollback budgets. Implementation: - lightweight prober module with opt-in sampling policy tied to rollout gates - reservoir-based diffusion estimator that adapts window size by observed burstiness - percentile residual smoother (EMA over 99th/95th) to avoid reacting to single-bucket spikes - integration layer with existing canary-evaluator and rollout-controller - feature flag and config knobs for dedicated vs shared pools Tests & validation: unit and integration tests for estimator math, simulations with synthetic burst traces, and a 48-hour production shadow run on staging showing 60% fewer unnecessary rollbacks and 30% lower 99th latency amplification during traffic bursts. Backwards compatibility: defaults are off; existing rollout logic remains unchanged unless feature flag enabled. Related: ENG-2381 (reduce noisy rollbacks), ENG-2399 (improve burst resilience). Checklist: [x] design doc linked [x] unit tests [x] integration tests [x] staging shadow run [x] dashboard and alert updates [x] docs update (console UI config).

title: Add canary scenarios for predictive autoscaling (traffic ramps + periodic spikes)
preview:
## Summary
Adds two new perf-canary workloads intended to catch latency regressions and oscillation when Dedicated predictive autoscaling is enabled:
1) `predictive_ramp_30m`: steady baseline then a monotonic ramp (to mimic “daily ramp” customers).
2) `predictive_spikes_10m`: periodic spikes with short recovery windows (to mimic bursty SaaS/cron-driven traffic).

These scenarios run against a Dedicated deployment with predictive mode toggled via canary config (feature-flagged per account) and record:
- p50/p95/p99 end-to-end latency
- queue depth (when available)
- autoscaler desired vs bounded desired replicas (to detect max-bound saturation and clamp behavior)
- scale event reasons + step sizes (to detect oscillation)
- prediction freshness + error (if forecast provider is enabled in the canary env)

The goal is to gate pilot expansions: predictive mode should not regress p95 during known ramps and should not introduce high-frequency scale-up/scale-down cycles under periodic spikes.

## Why
During the pilot we saw a case where predictive inputs + noisy saturation signals caused capacity jitter and increased latency during a customer ramp (ref: incident thread + follow-up hotfix in main autoscaler). This PR adds repeatable canary coverage so we can catch:
- predictor returning null/stale values leading to unsafe downscale (should fall back to reactive)
- oscillation due to overly-aggressive forecast deltas
- max-bound clamping causing sustained queue buildup (signal for misconfigured max or insufficient fleet capacity)

## What changed
- Added scenario definitions and traffic curves:
  - `scenarios/predictive_ramp_30m.yaml`
  - `scenarios/predictive_spikes_10m.yaml`
- Added thresholds + pass/fail gating:
  - p95 latency must remain within +10% of the reactive baseline run (same model + same request mix)
  - scale event frequency: no more than 6 scale actions / 10 min window after initial ramp stabilization
  - bounded-by-max detection: if `bounded_desired == max` for >5m AND queue depth > threshold => fail (signals capacity mismatch)
  - predictor staleness: if `forecast_age_seconds > 300` => mark predictive metrics as degraded and require reactive fallback tag
- Extended result emitter to include autoscaling “reason” tags in the summary artifacts (so we can tell “predictive_up” vs “reactive_saturation”).
- Added README notes for running locally against staging (requires canary service account with Dedicated read permissions).

## How to run
- CI: runs nightly and on-demand via `workflow_dispatch` (see `.github/workflows/predictive-autoscaling-canary.yml`).
- Local:
  - `CANARY_TARGET=dedicated-staging-us-west-2 CANARY_SCENARIO=predictive_ramp_30m make run`
  - `CANARY_TARGET=dedicated-staging-us-west-2 CANARY_SCENARIO=predictive_spikes_10m make run`

## Risk / rollout
Low risk: changes are isolated to perf-canary. The only operational coupling is metric name usage; this PR uses existing metric names emitted by the autoscaler and treats missing metrics as “skip” (non-fatal) to avoid breaking canary in regions that haven’t rolled out the new metrics yet.

## Checklist
- [x] Added scenario docs + example outputs
- [x] Verified canary runner handles missing prediction provider (marks run as reactive-only)
- [x] Validated thresholds on two replay runs against staging (reactive vs predictive) with no failures
- [x] Added artifact links in the workflow summary (Grafana + JSON summary)

## Example output (from staging)
- `predictive_ramp_30m`: p95 +3.2% vs reactive baseline; 3 scale actions after stabilization; no bound-hit.
- `predictive_spikes_10m`: p95 +5.8% vs reactive baseline; 5 scale actions; one brief clamp (<60s) without queue growth.

## Review notes / conversation
Rafael Mendes (SRE): Can we make the oscillation threshold model-aware? Some models have noisier token times and may drive more scale events.
Leila Farouk (author): Good call. For now this is pinned to the pilot model set in staging (same as Dedicated pilot). I added a config knob `max_scale_actions_per_10m` in the scenario YAML so we can tune per model when we expand coverage.

Vanessa Ortiz (Fleet): Please ensure clamp failures are actionable; we don’t want canary to fail just because max bound is intentionally low.
Leila Farouk (author): Updated clamp gating: it only fails when (a) clamped at max for >5m AND (b) queue depth exceeds the threshold (sustained queueing). If queue depth is unavailable, it logs a warning and does not hard-fail.

Sana Farid (Forecast eval): Can we record forecast freshness explicitly? We’ve had stale forecasts cause misleading results.
Leila Farouk (author): Added `forecast_age_seconds` and a “predictive_degraded” tag if age >300s. Also added a check that requires the runner to observe `reactive_fallback=true` if predictive is degraded.

Samantha Roy (Telemetry): Metric label cardinality—avoid adding customer_id/deployment_id to perf-canary summary.
Leila Farouk (author): Confirmed. The runner stores IDs only in the private run metadata artifact (restricted) and uses hashed identifiers in the public summary.

## CI
- canary-lint: pass
- unit (runner): pass
- scenario-validate: pass
- dry-run (no traffic): pass

## Merge
Squash merge into `main`.

Refs: ENG-22147 (load test + simulation), ENG-22154 (game day), ENG-22142 (forecast ingestion integration).

title: Rollout canary safety smoke tests (cohorting + auto-stop wiring) for staging
preview:
Summary: This PR adds a small suite of staging-only safety smoke tests for Hosted canary rollouts. The goal is to catch two classes of beta issues before they reach customers: (1) cohorting / split enforcement regressions (e.g., observed split mismatch per region) and (2) stop-condition wiring regressions (e.g., SLO breach evaluates but orchestrator doesn’t pause/rollback, or rollout step stalls on metric timeouts). These tests are intentionally narrow, deterministic, and cleanup-safe so they can be run as part of GA change management.

Motivation / context: During beta we saw an incident-like split mismatch in us-east (SUP-48377) and a separate class of failures where metric evaluation timeouts could stall progress (see follow-ups in ENG-9158 / pr-28895). We already have E2E coverage in the monorepo (ENG-9164), but we wanted a lightweight “canary of the canary” that runs against staging infrastructure end-to-end (routing + telemetry aggregation + orchestrator actions) without needing full Console UI flows.

What’s included: 1) Cohorting correctness smoke: Creates a temporary Hosted route + rollout with baseline/canary config, sets split to 20% canary, and sends N=2,000 synthetic requests with a fixed cohorting key distribution. Validates: (a) per-request cohort assignment is stable for the same key across retries, (b) overall split converges to expected bounds (20% ± 3% at N=2,000), (c) split is consistent across regions when the same key is used (us-east-1 + eu-west-1), and (d) streaming requests retain cohort stickiness across reconnect within the allowed retry window.

2) Stop-condition wiring smoke: Starts a rollout step and intentionally injects failures into the canary cohort only (via the staging failure-injection header understood by the Hosted gateway) to exceed the default stop threshold for 5xx within a short window. Validates: (a) telemetry tags include rollout_id and cohort and are queryable via the staging metrics API, (b) stop-condition evaluator fires and emits an action event, (c) orchestrator transitions the rollout into PAUSED or ROLLED_BACK per policy (configurable per test), and (d) no orphaned rollouts remain after test completion.

3) Anti-cardinality + safety rails: Tests do not create unbounded (rollout_id, route, region) series. The suite uses a single route per run with a unique suffix, but aggregates assertions via the orchestrator + a single metrics query path. Added guardrails that hard-fail if route names exceed a maximum length or if run attempts to create more than 2 rollouts.

4) Cleanup and time-bounding: Every test uses a strict max runtime with deferred cleanup. If a run times out, we still attempt to rollback/delete the rollout and delete the staging route. Cleanup is idempotent and safe to re-run.

Implementation details: - Uses the perf-canary harness to call control-plane and orchestrator endpoints directly (no Console dependency). - Adds a small wrapper for deterministic key generation to validate hash cohorting behavior. - Adds a metrics polling helper with exponential backoff and a hard cap; avoids busy-waiting. - Uses a single staging-only feature flag gate so the job is a no-op in non-staging environments.

Changed areas (high level): - tests/rollouts/test_rollout_split_accuracy.ts: new split validation test and cohort stickiness assertions - tests/rollouts/test_rollout_stop_condition.ts: new stop-condition wiring test with failure injection - lib/staging/orchestrator_client.ts: helper for rollout create/start/pause/rollback + state polling - lib/staging/metrics_client.ts: helper for cohort-tagged metrics queries with bounded polling - lib/staging/cleanup.ts: idempotent cleanup for routes + rollouts - ci/nightly.yml and ci/on_demand.yml: new job definition + required secrets

Illustrative snippet (cohort stickiness assertion): The test tracks (cohort, request_id) for a stable key and asserts that retries do not flip cohorts unless the rollout config changed. This mirrors the ADR decision for deterministic cohorting and protects against accidental inclusion of unstable fields in the hash input.

How to run: - On demand (staging): ./scripts/run_smoke_tests.sh --suite rollouts --env staging - CI: job “staging-rollout-smoke” (nightly) and optional manual dispatch

Rollout / risk: Low risk to production since this is perf-canary only. Risk to staging is mitigated via strict cleanup and caps. If staging is degraded, the suite is expected to fail fast and page nobody (non-paging); it will file a CI failure for visibility.

Follow-ups: - Consider adding a latency regression stop-condition test once default thresholds are finalized (ENG-9158). - Consider adding a “split mismatch per AZ” probe once we have better visibility into the per-AZ routing logs.

Checklist: - [x] Tests are time-bounded and cleanup-safe - [x] Does not increase metrics cardinality unboundedly - [x] Validates both routing enforcement and telemetry stop-condition wiring - [x] Documented required staging secrets - [x] Linked to relevant Linear + support ticket

title: Add predictive concurrency allocator with SLO-gating for canary routing
preview: Motivation: Canary evaluations often surface regressions caused by unexpected concurrency pressure on GPUs and model KV caches. This PR introduces a predictive concurrency allocator that uses short-window time-series features and a lightweight online estimator to predict occupancy and limit per-model concurrency before tail latency exceeds SLO thresholds. It ties into the existing canary pipeline so canaries can gate rollouts automatically when predicted saturation is likely. Design summary: - Runtime component: a new allocator service that subscribes to per-model throughput/latency streams and predicts short-term concurrency headroom using exponential smoothing and a small linear regressor. The allocator returns a recommended concurrency cap and a confidence score. - Canary integration: canary scheduler consults the allocator before opening traffic; if predicted tail-latency exceeds configured SLO margin the canary is paused and a soft rollback signal emitted. - Fallbacks: when the allocator is unavailable, we fall back to the static concurrency profiles. - Observability: new metrics (allocator_predicted_headroom, allocator_confidence, allocator_trigger_reason) and SLI dashboards added. - Config: exposed as a tunable in perf-canary Helm values with per-tenant overrides and a dry-run mode. Testing and validation: - Unit tests for estimator logic and boundary cases. - Integration tests in the perf-canary harness that replay historical traces and validate that the allocator reduces tail latency violations by ~35% in our samples. - Canary simulation suite to stress different traffic shapes (bursts, long tails, cold-start). Rollout plan: - Start in dry-run for 48h on canaries only (no gating). - Enable SLO gating for canaries with conservative thresholds for 2 weeks. - Gradually enable for low-risk models before wide rollout. Related work: builds on ENG-2134 (previous canary instrumentation) and complements the existing adaptive probe scheduler. Files changed (high level): list below details the main touched modules and rationale.

title: Add canary for telemetry query latency (incident-bot diagnostics guardrail)
preview:
## Context
Incident-bot v2 relies on the telemetry query service to render saturation/context cards in the first ~60–90s of an incident thread. We’ve seen query latency spikes during incidents (bursty usage + upstream timeouts) that cause empty/partial cards and slow responder workflows. This PR adds a dedicated perf canary to continuously validate telemetry query latency and error behavior under a representative diagnostic workload.

Related:
- INT-3341 (telemetry query timeouts from incident-bot)
- ENG-5427 (telemetry query cache/coalescing)
- ENG-5429 (bot degraded-mode + load shedding)

## What this adds
1) New canary: `telemetry-query-latency`
- Runs every 60s (configurable) from 3 regions: us-east-1, us-west-2, eu-west-1
- Executes a fixed set of low-cardinality queries that mirror incident-bot cards:
  - GPU utilization (pool-level, region scoped)
  - Queue depth / scheduler backpressure (service + region)
  - KV cache pressure (cluster + region)
  - Error budget burn link generator preflight query (metadata only)
- Includes a small amount of concurrency (default 4) to catch p95 regressions without introducing significant load

2) Success criteria + SLO-style thresholds
- Records latency histograms for each query family + overall request
- Enforces guardrails:
  - p95 < 2.0s for `telemetry_query_total`
  - p99 < 5.0s for `telemetry_query_total`
  - timeout rate < 1% over 10m
  - non-2xx rate < 0.5% over 10m
- Canary marks a run as failed if any threshold is violated; emits a structured failure reason to make debugging actionable (e.g., `upstream_timeout`, `rate_limited`, `bad_gateway`, `query_budget_exceeded`).

3) Metrics + dashboard wiring
Emits the following (Prom-style) metrics:
- `perf_canary_telemetry_query_latency_ms{region,query_family,tenant_scope}` (histogram)
- `perf_canary_telemetry_query_success_total{region,query_family}`
- `perf_canary_telemetry_query_failure_total{region,query_family,reason}`
- `perf_canary_telemetry_query_payload_bytes{region,query_family}` (to detect accidental query expansion)

Adds a lightweight dashboard panel set (documented in the runbook) and a deep-link format compatible with incident-bot cards.

4) Alert
Adds an alert intended for SRE + telemetry oncall:
- Fires when p95 or p99 breaches for 10m OR when timeout rate breaches for 10m
- Includes annotations for top failing query_family and region
- Routes to `#eng-sre` and `#telemetry-oncall` (same routing as other perf canaries)

## Implementation details
- Canary uses the same auth mechanism as other perf canaries, but is scoped to a synthetic service identity with read-only access to the telemetry query endpoint. No tenant/customer identifiers are used.
- Query parameters are explicitly bounded (time window 5m, region scoped, no per-tenant grouping) to avoid creating cardinality pressure.
- Adds backoff + jitter when a run fails (prevents thundering herd if telemetry service is unhealthy).
- Adds an allowlist of query templates to prevent accidental addition of ad-hoc expensive queries.

## Changed areas
- `canaries/telemetry_query_latency_canary.ts`: new canary implementation + query families
- `canaries/index.ts`: registry wiring
- `config/canaries.yaml`: scheduling + regions + thresholds
- `alerts/telemetry_query_latency.rules.yaml`: alert definitions
- `runbooks/telemetry-query-latency-canary.md`: triage steps and escalation pointers
- `dashboards/telemetry_query_latency_canary.json`: panels for latency/errors by region and query family

## Testing
- Unit tests for:
  - threshold evaluation and failure reason mapping
  - query template rendering and hard bounds (time window, grouping)
  - retry/backoff behavior
- Local dry-run: `yarn canary telemetry-query-latency --once --region us-east-1`

## Rollout plan
- Merge behind `canary.telemetryQueryLatency.enabled` (default off)
- Enable in staging for 24h, then prod in us-east-1 only for 24h
- Expand to remaining regions after confirming alert noise and baseline distributions

## Notes
This canary is intentionally conservative: it aims to detect when incident-bot context gathering is at risk, not to load test the telemetry query service. If we want load-based validation, we should add a separate stress canary with an explicit budget and ownership.

---
### Commits
- canary: add telemetry query latency canary scaffold
- canary: implement bounded query families (gpu/queue/kv/error-budget)
- metrics: add histograms + failure reason labels
- alerts: add telemetry query latency SLO alert
- docs: add runbook + dashboard references

### CI
- build (node 20): pass
- lint: pass
- unit tests: pass
- canary dry-run (staging): pass

### Merge
Squash merged into `main`.

---
### Review conversation highlights
Logan Wright: "Please double-check that the canary can’t accidentally introduce high-cardinality tags (tenant_id, model, route). We’ve been burned here." 
Nadia Rahman (author): "Added a hard allowlist for group-by keys and a unit test that fails if `tenant_id|model|route` appear in the rendered query. Also capped time window to 5m."

Allison Grant: "Alert will be noisy if we page on single-region transient spikes. Can we require 2/3 regions OR a 10m window?" 
Nadia Rahman (author): "Updated rule to 10m and added annotations for top region/query_family; kept per-region firing but with a 10m window and higher p99 threshold to reduce flaps."

Miles Thornton: "Can we include a hint in the alert on what incident-bot symptom this maps to?" 
Nadia Rahman (author): "Added alert annotation: 'Incident-bot diagnostics may degrade (empty/slow context cards). Consider enabling degraded-mode (links-only) if widespread.'"

Caleb Johnson: "Any risk that this canary masks real issues because it uses cache?" 
Nadia Rahman (author): "We explicitly set `cache_bypass=false` to match incident-bot behavior; the goal is end-user perceived latency. Added metric for payload_bytes + noted in runbook how to check uncached latency if debugging."
text:
## Context
Incident-bot v2 relies on the telemetry query service to render saturation/context cards in the first ~60–90s of an incident thread. We’ve seen query latency spikes during incidents (bursty usage + upstream timeouts) that cause empty/partial cards and slow responder workflows. This PR adds a dedicated perf canary to continuously validate telemetry query latency and error behavior under a representative diagnostic workload.

Related:
- INT-3341 (telemetry query timeouts from incident-bot)
- ENG-5427 (telemetry query cache/coalescing)
- ENG-5429 (bot degraded-mode + load shedding)

## What this adds
1) New canary: `telemetry-query-latency`
- Runs every 60s (configurable) from 3 regions: us-east-1, us-west-2, eu-west-1
- Executes a fixed set of low-cardinality queries that mirror incident-bot cards:
  - GPU utilization (pool-level, region scoped)
  - Queue depth / scheduler backpressure (service + region)
  - KV cache pressure (cluster + region)
  - Error budget burn link generator preflight query (metadata only)
- Includes a small amount of concurrency (default 4) to catch p95 regressions without introducing significant load

2) Success criteria + SLO-style thresholds
- Records latency histograms for each query family + overall request
- Enforces guardrails:
  - p95 < 2.0s for `telemetry_query_total`
  - p99 < 5.0s for `telemetry_query_total`
  - timeout rate < 1% over 10m
  - non-2xx rate < 0.5% over 10m
- Canary marks a run as failed if any threshold is violated; emits a structured failure reason to make debugging actionable (e.g., `upstream_timeout`, `rate_limited`, `bad_gateway`, `query_budget_exceeded`).

3) Metrics + dashboard wiring
Emits the following (Prom-style) metrics:
- `perf_canary_telemetry_query_latency_ms{region,query_family,tenant_scope}` (histogram)
- `perf_canary_telemetry_query_success_total{region,query_family}`
- `perf_canary_telemetry_query_failure_total{region,query_family,reason}`
- `perf_canary_telemetry_query_payload_bytes{region,query_family}` (to detect accidental query expansion)

Adds a lightweight dashboard panel set (documented in the runbook) and a deep-link format compatible with incident-bot cards.

4) Alert
Adds an alert intended for SRE + telemetry oncall:
- Fires when p95 or p99 breaches for 10m OR when timeout rate breaches for 10m
- Includes annotations for top failing query_family and region
- Routes to `#eng-sre` and `#telemetry-oncall` (same routing as other perf canaries)

## Implementation details
- Canary uses the same auth mechanism as other perf canaries, but is scoped to a synthetic service identity with read-only access to the telemetry query endpoint. No tenant/customer identifiers are used.
- Query parameters are explicitly bounded (time window 5m, region scoped, no per-tenant grouping) to avoid creating cardinality pressure.
- Adds backoff + jitter when a run fails (prevents thundering herd if telemetry service is unhealthy).
- Adds an allowlist of query templates to prevent accidental addition of ad-hoc expensive queries.

## Changed areas
- `canaries/telemetry_query_latency_canary.ts`: new canary implementation + query families
- `canaries/index.ts`: registry wiring
- `config/canaries.yaml`: scheduling + regions + thresholds
- `alerts/telemetry_query_latency.rules.yaml`: alert definitions
- `runbooks/telemetry-query-latency-canary.md`: triage steps and escalation pointers
- `dashboards/telemetry_query_latency_canary.json`: panels for latency/errors by region and query family

## Testing
- Unit tests for:
  - threshold evaluation and failure reason mapping
  - query template rendering and hard bounds (time window, grouping)
  - retry/backoff behavior
- Local dry-run: `yarn canary telemetry-query-latency --once --region us-east-1`

## Rollout plan
- Merge behind `canary.telemetryQueryLatency.enabled` (default off)
- Enable in staging for 24h, then prod in us-east-1 only for 24h
- Expand to remaining regions after confirming alert noise and baseline distributions

## Notes
This canary is intentionally conservative: it aims to detect when incident-bot context gathering is at risk, not to load test the telemetry query service. If we want load-based validation, we should add a separate stress canary with an explicit budget and ownership.

---
### Commits
- canary: add telemetry query latency canary scaffold
- canary: implement bounded query families (gpu/queue/kv/error-budget)
- metrics: add histograms + failure reason labels
- alerts: add telemetry query latency SLO alert
- docs: add runbook + dashboard references

### CI
- build (node 20): pass
- lint: pass
- unit tests: pass
- canary dry-run (staging): pass

### Merge
Squash merged into `main`.

---
### Review conversation highlights
Logan Wright: "Please double-check that the canary can’t accidentally introduce high-cardinality tags (tenant_id, model, route). We’ve been burned here." 
Nadia Rahman (author): "Added a hard allowlist for group-by keys and a unit test that fails if `tenant_id|model|route` appear in the rendered query. Also capped time window to 5m."

Allison Grant: "Alert will be noisy if we page on single-region transient spikes. Can we require 2/3 regions OR a 10m window?" 
Nadia Rahman (author): "Updated rule to 10m and added annotations for top region/query_family; kept per-region firing but with a 10m window and higher p99 threshold to reduce flaps."

Miles Thornton: "Can we include a hint in the alert on what incident-bot symptom this maps to?" 
Nadia Rahman (author): "Added alert annotation: 'Incident-bot diagnostics may degrade (empty/slow context cards). Consider enabling degraded-mode (links-only) if widespread.'"

Caleb Johnson: "Any risk that this canary masks real issues because it uses cache?" 
Nadia Rahman (author): "We explicitly set `cache_bypass=false` to match incident-bot behavior; the goal is end-user perceived latency. Added metric for payload_bytes + noted in runbook how to check uncached latency if debugging."

title: Add quantization profile canary scenarios (baseline vs profile-enabled) with thresholds + bisect hints
preview: Summary: This PR adds perf-canary coverage for the Quantization Profile Library route-level enablement by introducing paired scenarios that run baseline (default precision) vs profile-enabled execution for the same model/workload mix, then compares deltas against explicit thresholds. The goal is to catch (a) tail-latency regressions, (b) decode throughput drops, (c) VRAM/caching regressions, and (d) new error modes (profile incompatibility causing unexpected fallback churn), before profiles are exposed to beta traffic. Motivation: With route-level quantization enablement landing across control plane + runtime, we need deterministic, continuously-running signals that quantify the risk of enabling a profile and that produce actionable failure output. These scenarios are intended to run on the same infra as existing decode/tps canaries and to surface “bisect hints” that point engineers to likely layers (policy resolution vs artifact fetch vs kernel path vs cache pressure). What’s included: (1) A new scenario type `quant_profile_compare` that executes two runs (baseline + enabled) using identical prompts and compares p50/p95/p99 latency, tokens/sec, and error rate. (2) Scenario templates for two context regimes (short chat-style: 256 in / 256 out; long-context: 4k in / 512 out) to exercise KV cache behavior. (3) GPU matrix support (A100-80GB + H100-80GB) with per-SKU thresholds (H100 has stricter decode latency deltas due to kernel selection differences; A100 has stricter VRAM headroom thresholds). (4) Failure classification + automated bisect hints printed in the canary report, including common patterns: “metadata fetch spike / registry throttling”, “fallback rate elevated due to incompatibility”, “decode-only regression likely kernel path”, “prefill regression likely activation quantization / cache fragmentation”. (5) Wiring to export new comparison metrics so dashboards can slice by route/model/gpu and track trendlines over time. Notes/constraints: The scenarios default to running against a pinned model + pinned quantization_profile_ref in staging/perf-canary environments to avoid accidental drift. The scenario runner enforces determinism flags and pins seed where supported to reduce noise (still non-deterministic at token level; we only gate on perf/infra metrics). Thresholds were chosen based on last 14d baseline variance plus an additional buffer for multi-tenant noise; see `docs/quant_profile_compare.md` in this PR for rationale. Rollout: Add scenarios disabled-by-default behind `ENABLE_QUANT_PROFILE_CANARIES` until the control plane config endpoints are fully deployed in perf-canary. Once available, we’ll enable in staging for 48h, then production canary at low frequency before full schedule. Follow-ups: Add a third workload focused on tool-calling/structured output once the runtime path is fully aligned (out of scope here).

title: Add cache telemetry overhead perf-canary gate (p95 regression threshold)
preview:
Adds a new perf-canary workload and CI gate to quantify runtime overhead from the prefix/KV cache telemetry instrumentation. The canary runs a stable request mix with caching enabled and toggles telemetry flags to compare p95 end-to-end latency (prefill+decode) against a baseline. If the measured p95 regression exceeds the configured threshold, the canary fails to prevent rollouts that silently add latency overhead.

Motivation: We’re rolling out new cache metrics (hit/miss counters, eviction pressure, wasted prefill) behind flags and with sampling, but we need a hard stop in CI to keep instrumentation overhead near-zero (especially at p95/p99). This PR is intended to be the guardrail referenced by the runtime rollout plan and SRE approval thread.

What’s included: (1) New scenario `cache_telemetry_overhead` that replays a prompt set designed to exercise prefill, decode, prefix reuse, and steady-state KV reuse; includes prompt-length buckets (short/med/long) and a chat-like multi-turn loop to catch streaming-related paths. (2) A/B run harness: A = telemetry disabled, B = telemetry enabled at production defaults (sampling and rate limits on). Each arm is run N times and we take the median p95 to reduce flake. (3) Regression gate: fails if (B_p95 - A_p95) / A_p95 > 0.015 (1.5%) with a minimum absolute delta floor of 0.7ms to avoid noise-triggered failures on very fast hardware. (4) Artifacts: uploads JSON summary including p50/p95/p99, CPU util, and “telemetry event rate” counters emitted by the harness (not runtime metrics) so we can debug abnormal deltas. (5) Documentation: adds runbook-ish notes to the perf-canary README and exposes a single knob for temporarily widening the threshold via repo config (requires CODEOWNERS approval).

Notes/assumptions: This canary measures end-to-end request latency at the client boundary; it does not attempt to attribute time spent in metrics emission vs request processing. The goal is gating and early detection, not perfect profiling. The scenario uses deterministic seeds and fixed concurrency to minimize scheduler variance; it is pinned to a single GPU type in CI.

Rollout plan: Land in perf-canary first, run for 24h on main with threshold in “report-only” mode (configured via CI env var). Then flip to enforced mode once we confirm stability. This matches the broader cache telemetry rollout gate requirements (staging -> canary -> prod).

title: Add canary check for output-token metering parity (streaming + non-streaming)
preview:
Context: we’ve had at least one production regression where streaming responses under-counted output tokens in metering, which then cascaded into Unit Economics dashboards (blended $/1M output tokens) and reconciliation views. This PR adds an automated canary that detects output-token metering regressions before a gateway/runtime change is widely rolled out.

What this adds:
- New canary suite `metering_output_token_parity` that runs against a small allowlisted canary tenant in prod.
- Exercises BOTH streaming and non-streaming generation paths (including tool-call shaped outputs) and validates:
  1) output_tokens reported by the API response metadata
  2) output_tokens recorded by metering events (usage pipeline)
  3) that the delta between (1) and (2) is within a configured tolerance after accounting for ingestion latency
- Adds guardrails for ingestion lag: the canary polls the metering lookup API for up to 6 minutes (configurable) before declaring missing events.
- Emits a single structured result event per run (pass/fail + diagnostics) and wires alert routing:
  - warning to #eng-platform when parity drift > 0.5% for a single run
  - page oncall (telemetry) when drift > 1.0% for 3 consecutive runs OR when missing metering events exceed 2% of requests

Key implementation details:
- Request set: 18 requests per run (9 streaming, 9 non-streaming) with fixed prompt fixtures to keep variance low.
- Models: uses `rw-llama-3.1-8b-instruct` (cheap + stable) pinned to a specific model version for determinism.
- Tokenization alignment: uses the server-side token counts only (we do NOT attempt client-side tokenization); the canary is specifically checking parity between two server-side sources of truth.
- Correlation: uses `x-request-id` and `metering_correlation_id` to look up metering records; falls back to time window + route + model + canary tenant ID when correlation ID missing.
- Flake reduction: retries on 429/503 with exponential backoff; run is considered inconclusive (non-paging) if infra is degraded and error rate > 30%.

Rollout:
- Enabled in dry-run mode for 24h (records metrics but does not page).
- After 24h, flip `CANARY_METERING_PARITY_PAGING_ENABLED=true` in the perf-canary prod deployment.

Notes / limitations:
- This is not a billing reconciliation test. It only checks output token counts parity between API-side accounting and metering events.
- Known source of noise: very rare duplicate metering events; the canary de-dupes by (correlation_id, sequence).

Operational links:
- Runbook draft: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/unit-econ-output-token-parity (will be finalized alongside ENG-8435)

Why now:
- The internal Unit Economics dashboard (v1) depends on reliable output token counts (especially streaming); this canary is intended to catch regressions like the one fixed in `redwood` PR-19355 before they impact exec/finance reporting.

title: add-frequency-domain-residuals-detector-and-graceful-threshold-adapter
preview: Motivation: transient, periodic interference (GC sweeps, noisy neighbors, periodic telemetry jobs) has caused low-amplitude latency regressions that percentile-only detectors occasionally miss and that can trigger noisy rollbacks. This PR introduces a new detector that analyzes latency time-series in the frequency domain and fuses spectral residual signals with existing percentile drift metrics to produce a more stable canary signal. Design summary: 1) Windowed FFT transform of per-request p95/p99 time series over short sliding windows (configurable); 2) Compute spectral residual energy in targeted bands (e.g., 0.5-5s cycles) and normalize by traffic power to be traffic-aware; 3) Produce a residuals_score time series and combine with percentile delta via a probabilistic fusion step (logistic weight based on signal SNR); 4) Graceful Threshold Adapter: thresholds are scaled by a lightweight traffic intensity estimator and by recent variance to avoid over-reacting to cold-start or low-sample windows; 5) Rollout guard: when residuals_score exceeds adaptive threshold for N consecutive windows we pause canary traffic and open an investigation alert with suggested probes. Implementation notes: detector implemented in perf-canary/src/detectors/freq_residuals.py using numpy/scipy primitives with a fallback optimized CFFI path for high-throughput hosts; fusion and threshold adapter live in perf-canary/src/eval/fusion.py; added PromQL metric exporters and Grafana panel JSON for visibility under observability/canaries/freq-residuals-panel.json. Tests and validation: unit tests cover spectral normalization, SNR-based weighting, and adapter scaling (perf-canary/tests/test_freq_residuals.py); integration canary harness added to perf-canary/integration/canary-sim that replays synthetic traffic with injected periodic noise to validate true/false-positive curves; load impact: CPU microbenchmarks show ~2% extra CPU on probe workers, latency overhead negligible. Rollout plan: feature flag behind perf.canary.freq_residuals.enabled; start with passive mode (score logged but not gating) for 48h in perf environment, promote to active gating on a small percentage of canaries, then global default after two weeks if false-positive rate < 1% and detection recall improves. Related work: builds on ENG-2480 (spectral baseline) and complements existing percentile-aware detectors in this codebase. Backwards compatibility: detector registers as optional evaluation plugin; default off for existing canaries. Metrics to watch: residuals_score, fused_canary_signal, false_positive_rate, mean_time-to-pause, CPU impact. Operational playbook: added runbook section pointing to runbooks/canaries/freq-residuals-runbook.md with RCA steps and suggested probes. This PR includes code, tests, panels, and runbook entries. Merge notes: squash-merged to main after approvals and green CI.
